title: Momentum-gapped quasiparticles in disordered metals
summary: Nature contains massless particles with linear dispersions, and massive particles whose energies depend quadratically on their momenta with finite mass gaps. Both have equivalents in condensed matter physics in the form of collective modes and quasiparticles, measurable excitations with well-defined energy-momentum relations. A hypothesised third particle type - the super-luminal tachyon - would have an undefined energy at low momentum. A similar collective mode - long hypothesised within the hydrodynamic theory of matter - would have a purely imaginary energy at low momentum, corresponding to a finite lifetime. This third possibility has never been directly observed in a quantum system. Through a careful comparison of hydrodynamics with microscopic models of metals, we establish that this previously unseen third dispersion occurs in correlated quantum matter whenever the electronic fluid undergoes momentum relaxation due to explicit breaking of translation by impurities. As a specific example of these momentum-relaxed modes we consider the recent discovery of an acoustic plasmon - dubbed Pines' demon - in Sr$_2$RuO$_4$. The observed dispersion of this neutral mode differed significantly from the massless linear behaviour predicted by the random phase approximation. We demonstrate that the observed dispersion corresponds, in fact, to a momentum-gapped quasiparticle.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the first direct observation of a third particle type in quantum matter - momentum-relaxed collective modes with purely imaginary energy at low momentum, previously only theorized in hydrodynamic theory. Using Sr₂RuO₄'s acoustic plasmon (Pines' demon) as an example, the researchers showed this mode is actually a momentum-gapped quasiparticle rather than the massless linear dispersion predicted by conventional theory.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize a hypothesized third particle type in nature - one with purely imaginary energy at low momentum (corresponding to finite lifetime) - that has never been directly observed in quantum systems. This was motivated by anomalous experimental observations of acoustic plasmon dispersion in Sr₂RuO₄ that didn't match theoretical predictions.
AI.method: Through careful comparison of hydrodynamic theory with microscopic models of metals, the researchers established that this third dispersion type occurs when the electronic fluid undergoes momentum relaxation due to explicit breaking of translation symmetry by impurities.
AI.result: The third dispersion (momentum-relaxed mode) was found to occur in correlated quantum matter. The observed dispersion of Pines' demon in Sr₂RuO₄ was shown to correspond to a momentum-gapped quasiparticle, significantly differing from the massless linear behavior predicted by the random phase approximation (RPA).
AI.conclusion: The previously unseen third dispersion - the momentum-relaxed mode with purely imaginary energy at low momentum - has been directly observed in Sr₂RuO₄. This represents the first experimental confirmation of a particle type that was long hypothesised within hydrodynamic theory but never directly seen in a quantum system.

title: Luttinger's Theorem Violation and Green's Function Topological Invariants in a Fractional Chern Insulator
summary: Luttinger's theorem constrains the particle density of interacting fermions through global properties of the single-particle Green's function, and its violation signals a breakdown of the identification between the quantized Hall response and the Green-function-based Ishikawa-Matsuyama invariant. This phenomenon becomes especially compelling in strongly correlated topological phases, such as fractional Chern insulators, where fractionalized quasiparticles lack an adiabatic connection to electrons, raising the question of how Green's-function-based topological invariants manifest in such phases. Using exact diagonalization of the fermionic Harper-Hofstadter-Hubbard model, we compute bulk single-particle Green's functions deep inside a fractional Chern insulating phase and directly evaluate the Luttinger count, its possible correction (the Luttinger integral), and the Ishikawa-Matsuyama invariant $N_3[\mathrm{G}]$. We demonstrate a clear violation of Luttinger's theorem and show that the fractional nature of the many-body Chern number is encoded in the Středa response of the Luttinger integral, while the integer invariant $N_3[\mathrm{G}]$ arises from the Středa response of the Luttinger count. We also analytically prove that $N_3[\mathrm{G}]$ is fully determined by the Luttinger count together with the Chern number of the occupied Bloch band, upon neglecting Bloch-band mixing. Finally, we propose an experimental protocol to extract all Green-function-based topological invariants from local density-of-states measurements, experimentally accessible in fractional quantum Hall systems.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the violation of Luttinger's theorem in fractional Chern insulators, demonstrating how Green's-function-based topological invariants (particularly the Ishikawa-Matsuyama invariant N₃[G]) encode fractional topological order when standard electron-like quasiparticles break down.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how Green's-function-based topological invariants manifest in strongly correlated topological phases where fractionalized quasiparticles lack adiabatic connections to electrons. The key question is how the fractional nature of topological order is encoded when traditional bulk-boundary correspondences fail.
AI.method: The authors use exact diagonalization of the fermionic Harper-Hofstadter-Hubbard model to compute bulk single-particle Green's functions deep inside a fractional Chern insulating phase. They directly evaluate the Luttinger count, the Luttinger integral (possible correction), and the Ishikawa-Matsuyama invariant N₃[G]. They also perform analytical proofs regarding the relationship between N₃[G] and other topological quantities.
AI.result: The authors demonstrate a clear violation of Luttinger's theorem and show that the fractional nature of the many-body Chern number is encoded in the Středa response of the Luttinger integral, while the integer invariant N₃[G] arises from the Středa response of the Luttinger count. They prove that N₃[G] is fully determined by the Luttinger count together with the Chern number of the occupied Bloch band (when Bloch-band mixing is neglected).
AI.conclusion: The key conclusion is that N₃[G] remains an integer despite Luttinger's theorem violation, with the fractional topological information instead captured by the Středa response of the Luttinger integral. The authors also propose an experimental protocol to extract all Green-function-based topological invariants from local density-of-states measurements, which is experimentally accessible in fractional quantum Hall systems.

title: Field-direction sensitivity of Kondo hybridization in UTe$_2$
summary: Neutron scattering experiments on the spin-triplet superconductor UTe$_2$ have established that the dominant low-energy magnetic response is along Brillouin zone boundaries, resembling the magnetic susceptibility of narrow-gap interband excitations. We report a study of the sensitivity of these excitations to magnetic field along the crystallographic $\hat{a}$-axis. Up to fields of $μ_0 H$=13 T, the maximal inelastic neutron spectral weight increases in energy transfer, with a pronounced increase in $d\hbarω_{peak}/dH$ near $μ_0 H$=7 T. This behavior parallels the field and temperature dependent features of the electrical resistivity that are associated with Kondo hybridization. Our measurements suggest that $\hat{a}$-axis fields near $μ_0 H$=7~T induce a change in the hybridization between heavy $f$-electrons and the bare conduction band.

AI.tldr: Neutron scattering experiments on the spin-triplet superconductor UTe2 reveal that low-energy magnetic excitations along Brillouin zone boundaries increase in energy with applied magnetic field along the â-axis, with a pronounced change occurring around μ0H = 7 T, suggesting field-induced changes in f-electron conduction band hybridization.
AI.motivation: Understanding the nature of magnetic excitations and their field sensitivity in spin-triplet superconductors like UTe2, particularly as they relate to Kondo hybridization effects observed in transport measurements.
AI.method: Inelastic neutron scattering experiments with magnetic field applied along the crystallographic â-axis up to 13 T, measuring the field dependence of magnetic spectral weight and peak energy.
AI.result: The maximal inelastic neutron spectral weight increases with field, showing a pronounced increase in dℏωpeak/dH near μ0H = 7 T, paralleling resistivity features associated with Kondo hybridization.
AI.conclusion: â-axis magnetic fields near 7 T induce a change in hybridization between heavy f-electrons and the conduction band in UTe2.

title: Magnetic-field tuning of the spin dynamics in the quasi-2D van der Waals antiferromagnet CuCrP$_{2}$S$_{6}$
summary: The use of antiferromagnets in magnetoelectronic devices as counterparts of ferromagnets is a new, rapidly developing trend in spintronics that leverages antiferromagnetic (AFM) magnons for transmitting of spin currents. Van der Waals (vdW) antiferromagnets are particularly attractive in this respect as they possess tunable magnetic properties and can be easily integrated into spintronic devices. In this work we use electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy to assess the potential of the vdW AFM compound CuCrP$_{2}$S$_{6}$ for magnonic applications by exploring the magnetic field ($H$) dependence of the spectrum of magnon excitations below its AFM ordering temperature $T_{\rm N} \approx 30$ K and the correlated spin dynamics above $T_{\rm N}$. ESR reveals prominent ferromagnetic (FM) spin correlations that persist far above $T_{\rm N}$ suggesting an intrinsically two-dimensional character of the spin dynamics in CuCrP$_{2}$S$_{6}$. Most interestingly, at $T < T_{\rm N}$, CuCrP$_{2}$S$_{6}$ features two non-degenerate, i.e., distinct in energy AFM magnon modes at $H = 0$ which can be tuned to the FM type of collective spin excitations with increasing $H$. These remarkable properties are favorable for the induction and control of unidirectional spin current in CuCrP$_{2}$S$_{6}$ and suggest it as a new functional material for magnetoelectronics.

AI.tldr: This study investigates CuCrP2S6, a van der Waals antiferromagnet, for magnonic applications using electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. The material shows two distinct AFM magnon modes at zero field that can be tuned to FM-type excitations with applied magnetic field, making it promising for spintronic devices.
AI.motivation: Antiferromagnets are emerging as promising alternatives to ferromagnets in spintronic devices for transmitting spin currents. Van der Waals antiferromagnets are particularly attractive due to their tunable magnetic properties and easy integration into devices. This work aims to evaluate CuCrP2S6's potential for magnonic applications by studying its spin dynamics.
AI.method: Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was used to explore the magnetic field dependence of magnon excitations in CuCrP2S6 below its AFM ordering temperature (TN ≈ 30 K) and the correlated spin dynamics above TN.
AI.result: ESR revealed prominent ferromagnetic spin correlations persisting far above TN, suggesting an intrinsically two-dimensional character of spin dynamics. Most notably, at T < TN, CuCrP2S6 exhibits two non-degenerate AFM magnon modes at H = 0 that can be tuned to FM-type collective spin excitations with increasing magnetic field.
AI.conclusion: The remarkable properties of CuCrP2S6, including tunable magnon modes and two-dimensional spin dynamics, are favorable for inducing and controlling unidirectional spin current, making it a promising new functional material for magnetoelectronics.

title: Real-space microscopic description of laser-pulse induced melting of superconductivity
summary: Quenching quantum order via laser pulses has proven a useful tool to access exotic physical effects in systems that are strongly perturbed out of equilibrium. However, theoretical modelling of experimental measurements is typically done phenomenologically or by assuming translational invariance due to the complexity of the problem. Here, we solve a microscopic real-space model of the time dynamics of a superconductor following an intense laser-pulse. We are able to reproduce recent experimental findings displaying a critical slowing-down of the melting of the order parameter for laser fluences close to the condensation energy. Moreover, we leverage the real-space resolution of our model to predict how phase fluctuations and currents in the system behave both spatially and temporally. We discover an unusual current flow in the superconductor after the pulse has subsided, resembling backward waves that normally require special engineering in metamaterials or wave guides. Our results predict a rich behavior of the superconducting order parameter at a microscopic level which is manifested in current textures that can be probed using radiation detection.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a microscopic real-space model to simulate superconductor dynamics after intense laser-pulse quenching, successfully reproducing experimental critical slowing-down phenomena and predicting unusual backward wave-like current patterns.
AI.motivation: Theoretical modeling of laser-pulse quenched superconductors is typically done phenomenologically or with simplified assumptions due to complexity. This work aims to develop a more accurate microscopic real-space approach.
AI.method: The authors solve a microscopic real-space model of superconductor time dynamics following intense laser-pulse excitation, leveraging real-space resolution to track spatial and temporal behavior of phase fluctuations and currents.
AI.result: The model reproduces experimental findings of critical slowing-down of order parameter melting near condensation energy fluences. Additionally, the model predicts unusual backward wave-like current flows in the superconductor after the pulse, a phenomenon typically requiring special engineering in metamaterials.
AI.conclusion: The results reveal rich microscopic behavior of superconducting order parameters manifested in current textures that can be probed via radiation detection, offering new insights into non-equilibrium superconductor dynamics.

title: Machine intelligence supports the full chain of 2D dendrite synthesis
summary: Exemplified by the chemical vapor deposition growth of two-dimensional dendrites, which has potential applications in catalysis and presents a parameter-intensive, data-scarce and reaction process-complex model problem, we devise a machine intelligence-empowered framework for the full chain support of material synthesis, encompassing rapid process optimization, accurate customized synthesis, and comprehensive mechanism deciphering.First, active learning is integrated into the experimental workflow, identifying an optimal recipe for the growth of highly-branched, electrocatalytically-active ReSe2 dendrites through 60 experiments (4 iterations), which account for less than 1.3% of the numerous possible parameter combinations.Then, a prediction accuracy-guided data augmentation strategy is developed combined with a tree-based machine learning (ML) algorithm, unveiling a non-linear correlation between 5 process variables and fractal dimension (DF) of ReSe2 dendrites with only 9 experiment additions, which guides the synthesis of various user-defined DF. Finally, we construct a data-knowledge dual-driven mechanism model by integration of cross-scale characterizations, interpretable ML models, and domain knowledge in thermodynamics and kinetics, unraveling synergistic contributions of multiple process parameters to the product morphology. This work demonstrates the ML potential to transform the research paradigm and is adaptable to broader material synthesis.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a machine intelligence framework for material synthesis optimization, demonstrated through chemical vapor deposition growth of ReSe2 dendrites. The approach combines active learning, prediction-guided data augmentation, and dual-driven mechanism modeling to achieve rapid process optimization, accurate customized synthesis, and mechanism deciphering.
AI.motivation: The chemical vapor deposition growth of 2D dendrites represents a parameter-intensive, data-scarce, and complex process problem with applications in catalysis. Traditional trial-and-error approaches are inefficient due to the vast parameter space, necessitating a machine learning-based framework for accelerated discovery and optimization.
AI.method: The framework integrates three key components: (1) Active learning integrated into experimental workflow for rapid process optimization; (2) Prediction accuracy-guided data augmentation combined with tree-based machine learning algorithms; (3) Data-knowledge dual-driven mechanism model integrating cross-scale characterizations, interpretable ML models, and domain knowledge in thermodynamics and kinetics.
AI.result: Through only 60 experiments (4 iterations, <1.3% of possible combinations), the framework identified optimal recipes for highly-branched, electrocatalytically-active ReSe2 dendrites. With just 9 additional experiments, the team uncovered non-linear correlations between 5 process variables and fractal dimension, enabling synthesis of user-defined dendrite structures. The mechanism model revealed synergistic contributions of multiple parameters to product morphology.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the transformative potential of machine learning in materials science, establishing a generalizable framework adaptable to broader material synthesis challenges. The approach successfully transforms research paradigms by enabling efficient exploration of vast parameter spaces while providing mechanistic insights.

title: Generalized symmetry-protected topological phases in mixed states from gauging dualities
summary: Decoherence in realistic quantum platforms motivates a mixed-state notion of topological phases of matter, including average symmetry-protected topological (ASPT) phases. Alongside this progress, generalized symmetries--notably noninvertible and dipole symmetries--have become powerful organizing principles for exotic quantum phases, yet their implications for mixed states remain less explored. In this work, we bridge these directions through a gauging correspondence between mixed-state phases with generalized symmetries and mixed-state phases with ordinary group symmetries, recasting the classification of noninvertible and dipole ASPT phases into familiar classifications of symmetry breaking and ASPT phases with dual symmetries. Using this approach, we classify and construct a subclass of ASPT phases with non-invertible and dipole symmetries in $(1+1)d$, including phases that are intrinsic to mixed states, and characterize them via string order parameters and protected edge modes.

AI.tldr: This paper bridges generalized symmetries (noninvertible and dipole) with mixed-state topological phases by establishing a gauging correspondence that recasts these exotic phases into familiar classifications. It classifies ASPT phases with these generalized symmetries in 1+1D, including intrinsically mixed-state phases, and characterizes them via string order parameters and protected edge modes.
AI.motivation: Decoherence in realistic quantum platforms creates mixed-state topological phases, while generalized symmetries provide powerful organizing principles for exotic quantum phases - but their combination remains underexplored. The authors aim to bridge these two directions through a gauging correspondence.
AI.method: The authors use a gauging correspondence between mixed-state phases with generalized symmetries and mixed-state phases with ordinary group symmetries. This approach recasts the classification of noninvertible and dipole ASPT phases into familiar classifications of symmetry breaking and ASPT phases with dual symmetries.
AI.result: The paper presents a classification and construction of a subclass of ASPT phases with non-invertible and dipole symmetries in (1+1)d, including phases that are intrinsic to mixed states (not realizable in pure states). These phases are characterized via string order parameters and protected edge modes.
AI.conclusion: The gauging correspondence provides a systematic framework for understanding mixed-state phases with generalized symmetries, successfully classifying and constructing ASPT phases with noninvertible and dipole symmetries in 1+1D, with characterizations through string order parameters and protected edge modes.

title: Interplay of superconductivity and ferromagnetism in ferromagnetic semiconductor-based Josephson junctions
summary: The interplay between superconductivity and ferromagnetism has long been pursued as a route to unconventional Josephson effects, yet suitable material platforms remain limited. Here we report Josephson junctions based on epitaxial Al/InAs/(Ga,Fe)Sb heterostructures grown by low-temperature molecular beam epitaxy, achieving atomically abrupt superconductor/semiconductor/ferromagnetic interfaces. The devices exhibit clear proximity-induced superconductivity, including multiple Andreev reflections and gate-tunable supercurrents, confirming transparent coupling across the hybrid structure. Under perpendicular magnetic fields, the junctions reveal highly unconventional Fraunhofer interference patterns with hysteresis, flux jumps, asymmetric lobe evolution, and clear nonreciprocity, providing strong evidence of induced ferromagnetism and broken time-reversal symmetry in the superconducting channel. Gate control further modulates the critical current, highlighting the semiconducting nature of the system. Our results demonstrate that ferromagnetic semiconductor heterostructures can serve as a highly tunable platform for exploring proximity-induced superconductivity and superconducting diode effects, and for advancing device concepts at the intersection of magnetism and quantum electronics.

AI.tldr: This paper reports Josephson junctions using epitaxial Al/InAs/(Ga,Fe)Sb heterostructures with atomically abrupt interfaces, demonstrating proximity-induced superconductivity with multiple Andreev reflections and gate-tunable supercurrents. Unconventional Fraunhofer patterns under perpendicular magnetic fields reveal hysteresis, flux jumps, and nonreciprocity, providing evidence of induced ferromagnetism and broken time-reversal symmetry.
AI.motivation: The interplay between superconductivity and ferromagnetism offers a route to unconventional Josephson effects, but suitable material platforms have been limited. This work aims to develop a tunable platform combining these competing order parameters.
AI.method: The researchers grew epitaxial Al/InAs/(Ga,Fe)Sb heterostructures using low-temperature molecular beam epitaxy to achieve atomically abrupt superconductor/semiconductor/ferromagnetic interfaces. They fabricated Josephson junction devices and characterized them using transport measurements under perpendicular magnetic fields and gate voltage tuning.
AI.result: The devices show clear proximity-induced superconductivity with multiple Andreev reflections and gate-tunable supercurrents. Under perpendicular magnetic fields, highly unconventional Fraunhofer interference patterns appear with hysteresis, flux jumps, asymmetric lobe evolution, and clear nonreciprocity, indicating induced ferromagnetism and broken time-reversal symmetry. Gate control effectively modulates the critical current.
AI.conclusion: Ferromagnetic semiconductor heterostructures can serve as a highly tunable platform for exploring proximity-induced superconductivity and superconducting diode effects, advancing device concepts at the intersection of magnetism and quantum electronics.

title: From pore collapse to crystal growth: ultrafast laser-induced stishovite formation in nanoporous silica
summary: The crystallization of amorphous solids under ultrafast laser irradiation represents a paradigm of non-equilibrium phase transitions, where the interplay between electromagnetic energy localization and atomic-scale dynamics remains largely uncharted. By using a multiscale framework that couples Finite-Difference Time-Domain simulations of nonlinear light propagation with Molecular Dynamics of the atomic response, we demonstrate that field enhancement at nanopore interfaces confines laser energy and drives a rapid collapse of the surrounding matrix. In the silica structure containing a nanopore of 2 nm radius, corresponding to a porosity of approximately 7%, the enhanced local electromagnetic field led to a final equilibrium temperature 16% higher than for the 1-nm pore (1% porosity), and 20% higher than for the homogeneous medium. Particularly, the heterogeneous energy localization in the 2-nm-pore system created a preferential nucleation site within the dense glass network and led to the ultrafast formation of stishovite, a high-pressure crystalline phase of SiO2, on a sub-nanosecond timescale, significantly faster than in homogeneous silica at the same equilibrium temperature. Such accelerated crystallization enables the phase transition to outpace pressure relaxation, which would otherwise inhibit stishovite formation under identical thermal loading. These predictions align with experimental observations of laser-induced crystallization in confined geometries and establish nanopores as potent catalysts for controlling solid-state transformations via tailored electromagnetic hotspots.

AI.tldr: Multiscale simulations reveal that nanopores in silica glass act as electromagnetic hotspots under ultrafast laser irradiation, concentrating energy to drive ultrafast formation of stishovite (high-pressure SiO2 phase) on sub-nanosecond timescales, significantly faster than in homogeneous silica.
AI.motivation: The interplay between electromagnetic energy localization and atomic-scale dynamics in crystallization of amorphous solids under ultrafast laser irradiation remains poorly understood. This study aims to explore how nanopores can catalyze solid-state phase transitions.
AI.method: Multiscale framework coupling Finite-Difference Time-Domain (FDTD) simulations of nonlinear light propagation with Molecular Dynamics (MD) of atomic response. Silica structures with nanopores of different sizes (1 nm and 2 nm radius) were studied.
AI.result: The 2-nm-pore system (7% porosity) showed 16% higher equilibrium temperature than the 1-nm pore (1% porosity) and 20% higher than homogeneous medium. The heterogeneous energy localization created preferential nucleation sites, leading to ultrafast stishovite formation on sub-nanosecond timescale—significantly faster than homogeneous silica at identical temperature.
AI.conclusion: Nanopores act as potent catalysts for controlling solid-state transformations via tailored electromagnetic hotspots. This enables crystallization to outpace pressure relaxation, which would otherwise inhibit stishovite formation. These predictions align with experimental observations of laser-induced crystallization in confined geometries.

title: Novel Magnetoacoustic Resonance Technique for Exploring Hidden Quadrupoles in a Crystal Field Quartet
summary: Crystal field quartets with quadrupole degrees of freedom play a crucial role in hidden ordering systems, as exemplified by CeB$_6$. We present a novel magnetoacoustic resonance technique that combines acoustically induced strain fields with a linearly polarized high-frequency microwave field to probe quadrupoles inherent in the quartet hidden behind magnetic properties. This method offers the advantage of enabling quantum quadrupole resonance transitions for large excitation energy gaps within quartet sublevels under a strong magnetic field, which cannot be achieved by acoustic experiments alone. Formulating a simultaneous single-phonon-single-photon absorption transition process using Floquet theory, we demonstrate how the transition probabilities are affected by changing the propagation direction of a bulk acoustic wave. The key result is that distinct maxima in transition probabilities, attributed to specific propagation directions, indicate a characteristic of quadrupole physics and exhibit an abrupt change owing to an induced ordered moment. This photon-assisted magnetoacoustic resonance technique will promote a broader range of applications of acoustic experiments for the study of quadrupole physics.

AI.tldr: A novel magnetoacoustic resonance technique is developed to probe quadrupole degrees of freedom in crystal field quartet systems (like CeB6), combining acoustic strain fields with linearly polarized high-frequency microwave fields to enable quantum quadrupole resonance transitions that cannot be achieved by acoustic experiments alone.
AI.motivation: Hidden ordering systems with crystal field quartots and quadrupole degrees of freedom (e.g., CeB6) are challenging to study because quadrupole properties are often masked by magnetic properties. Traditional acoustic experiments cannot access large excitation energy gaps within quartet sublevels under strong magnetic fields, necessitating a new experimental approach.
AI.method: The technique combines acoustically induced strain fields with linearly polarized high-frequency microwave fields. Simultaneous single-phonon-single-photon absorption transition processes are formulated using Floquet theory. The method probes how transition probabilities vary with the propagation direction of bulk acoustic waves.
AI.result: Distinct maxima in transition probabilities occur at specific propagation directions, which are characteristic of quadrupole physics. These maxima exhibit abrupt changes due to induced ordered moments, providing a signature of quadrupole behavior.
AI.conclusion: This photon-assisted magnetoacoustic resonance technique significantly expands the applicability of acoustic experiments for studying quadrupole physics in hidden ordering systems, enabling exploration of larger energy gaps and stronger magnetic fields than previously possible.

title: $\textit{Ab initio}$ Identification of Hydrogen Tunneling as Two-Level Systems in Nb$_2$O$_5$ and Ta$_2$O$_5$
summary: Two-level systems (TLS) in native Nb and Ta oxides limit superconducting-qubit coherence and SRF-cavity quality factors in the microwave frequency range, yet their microscopic origin remains unclear. We combine MLIP-accelerated sampling of hydrogen configurations and diffusion pathways in amorphous Nb and Ta pentoxides with targeted $\textit{ab initio}$ validation. Hydrogen is the only light interstitial with barrier-distance combinations near the $\sim0.1-10$ GHz tunneling regime, and its ensemble statistics in amorphous oxides produce effective TLS densities and loss estimates consistent with the experimentally observed higher loss in Nb oxide than in Ta oxide. Our results point to H tunneling as a plausible microscopic TLS source in these materials.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the microscopic origin of Two-level systems (TLS) in native Nb and Ta pentoxides that limit superconducting qubit coherence and SRF cavity performance. Using machine learning interatomic potential (MLIP)-accelerated sampling and ab initio calculations, the study identifies hydrogen tunneling as the likely source of TLS, with ensemble statistics matching experimental observations showing higher loss in Nb oxide than Ta oxide.
AI.motivation: Two-level systems (TLS) in amorphous Nb and Ta oxides are a major problem for superconducting qubits and SRF cavities, causing decoherence and quality factor degradation in the microwave frequency range. Despite extensive experimental observation, the microscopic origin of these TLS remained unclear, which motivated this investigation.
AI.method: The researchers combined MLIP-accelerated sampling of hydrogen configurations and diffusion pathways in amorphous Nb and Ta pentoxides with targeted ab initio validation to study potential TLS candidates.
AI.result: Hydrogen was identified as the only light interstitial with barrier-distance combinations in the ~0.1-10 GHz tunneling regime. Ensemble statistics of hydrogen in amorphous oxides produce effective TLS densities and loss estimates consistent with experimentally observed higher loss in Nb oxide compared to Ta oxide.
AI.conclusion: H tunneling represents a plausible microscopic TLS source in these materials, providing a potential explanation for qubit decoherence and cavity loss phenomena.

title: Accelerating Structure-Property Relationship Discovery with Multimodal Machine Learning and Self-Driving Microscopy
summary: Microscopy combined with local spectroscopy is widely used to correlate nanoscale structure with functional properties in materials, but conventional measurements rely heavily on human-selected sampling locations and predefined targets, limiting dataset diversity and the potential for discovery. Here, we present a framework that integrates autonomous microscopy with a dual-novelty deep kernel learning (DN-DKL) for adaptive data acquisition and a dual variational autoencoder (VAE) for representation learning. DN-DKL actively guides the microscopy toward structurally and spectroscopically novel regions, enabling efficient collection of large spectral datasets. Dual-VAE embeds local structure and spectroscopic responses into a shared latent manifold that serves as a structure-property relationship map. We applied this framework for the investigation of halide perovskite films using conductive atomic force microscopy. The results reveal distinct hysteresis behaviors that are linked to specific nanoscale structural motifs, including grain boundary junction points that show hysteresis under different bias conditions and asymmetric grain boundaries that suppress charge transport. This framework establishes a general strategy that leverages the complementary strengths of self-driving microscopy, machine learning, and human expertise to accelerate scientific discovery in functional materials.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an autonomous microscopy framework combining dual-novelty deep kernel learning (DN-DKL) and dual variational autoencoder (VAE) to enable adaptive data acquisition and representation learning for investigating halide perovskite films. The framework actively identifies structurally and spectroscopically novel regions, revealing distinct hysteresis behaviors linked to nanoscale structural motifs like grain boundary junctions and asymmetric grain boundaries.
AI.motivation: Conventional microscopy combined with local spectroscopy relies heavily on human-selected sampling locations and predefined targets, which limits dataset diversity and the potential for scientific discovery in functional materials research.
AI.method: The framework integrates three key components: (1) Autonomous microscopy for automated data collection, (2) Dual-novelty deep kernel learning (DN-DKL) that actively guides microscopy toward structurally and spectroscopically novel regions for efficient spectral dataset collection, and (3) Dual variational autoencoder (VAE) that embeds local structure and spectroscopic responses into a shared latent manifold representing structure-property relationships. Applied to halide perovskite films using conductive atomic force microscopy (c-AFM).
AI.result: The framework revealed distinct hysteresis behaviors linked to specific nanoscale structural motifs: (1) grain boundary junction points showing hysteresis under different bias conditions, and (2) asymmetric grain boundaries that suppress charge transport.
AI.conclusion: This framework establishes a general strategy leveraging the complementary strengths of self-driving microscopy, machine learning, and human expertise to accelerate scientific discovery in functional materials by efficiently exploring novel regions and mapping structure-property relationships.

title: Exactly Solvable Disorder-free Quantum Breakdown Model: Spectrum, Thermodynamics, and Dynamics
summary: We introduce and study a disorder-free version of the quantum breakdown model with all-to-all interactions. The Hamiltonian factorizes into the product of the zero-momentum-mode occupation number and a quadratic Hamiltonian including only pairing terms. This structure makes the model exactly solvable and produces a large set of zero-energy states. We analyze its spectral, thermodynamic, and dynamical properties. In particular, we show how the factorized structure shapes the spectral form factor and the real-time dynamics. We also compute two-point functions and out-of-time-ordered correlators (OTOCs), and find a distinct early-time growth regime in the OTOCs. These results provide a solvable setting in which spectral properties and real-time dynamics can be analyzed in a controlled way in the absence of disorder, spatial structure, and environmental coupling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a disorder-free quantum breakdown model with all-to-all interactions that is exactly solvable due to its factorized Hamiltonian structure, allowing analysis of spectral properties, thermodynamics, and real-time dynamics including OTOCs.
AI.motivation: To provide a solvable model for studying spectral properties and real-time dynamics in quantum systems without disorder, spatial structure, or environmental coupling.
AI.method: The model Hamiltonian is constructed to factorize into the product of zero-momentum-mode occupation number and a quadratic Hamiltonian with only pairing terms, enabling exact solutions.
AI.result: The factorized structure shapes the spectral form factor and real-time dynamics. Two-point functions and OTOCs were computed, revealing a distinct early-time growth regime in OTOCs.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a controlled, solvable setting for analyzing spectral properties and real-time dynamics in the absence of disorder, spatial structure, and environmental coupling.

title: A Dayem Loop Qubit Based on Interfering Superconducting Nanowires
summary: We propose a qubit design based on two parallel superconducting nanowires (i.e., a "Dayem loop qubit"). The inclusion of two nanowires instead of one leads to the Little-Parks effect, which provides an oscillator behavior for the qubit frequency as well as anharmonicity. Our key result is that even if the nanowires have an increasingly linear CPR at low supercurrents, the quantum interference between two condensates, induced by a magnetic field, leads to a restoration of cubic nonlinearity, which is predicted to be sufficient to create a functional transmon qubit based on thin superconducting wires. We consider both generic (cubic) current-phase relationships (CPR) as well as more realistic microscopic CPR, having higher-order nonlinearities. For higher-order CPRs, we propose a simple power-law phenomenological approximation valid at very low temperatures, at which superconducting qubits normally operate.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a "Dayem loop qubit" design using two parallel superconducting nanowires. The quantum interference between two condensates induced by a magnetic field restores cubic nonlinearity, enabling functional transmon qubits based on thin superconducting wires.
AI.motivation: Traditional transmon qubits rely on Josephson junctions with intrinsic nonlinear current-phase relationships (CPR). This work seeks an alternative approach using thin superconducting nanowires, which have increasingly linear CPR at low supercurrents, potentially losing the nonlinearity needed for qubit operation. The motivation is to restore the necessary cubic nonlinearity through quantum interference between two condensates.
AI.method: The study uses two parallel superconducting nanowires (Dayem loop) to exploit the Little-Parks effect, which provides oscillator behavior and anharmonicity. The researchers analyze both generic cubic CPRs and microscopic CPRs with higher-order nonlinearities, proposing a power-law phenomenological approximation valid at very low temperatures.
AI.result: The key finding is that quantum interference between two condensates induced by a magnetic field restores cubic nonlinearity, even when nanowires have nearly linear CPR at low supercurrents. This restored nonlinearity is predicted to be sufficient for creating functional transmon qubits based on thin superconducting wires.
AI.conclusion: The Dayem loop qubit design offers a viable alternative to traditional Josephson junction-based transmon qubits, leveraging thin superconducting nanowires with restored cubic nonlinearity through quantum interference, potentially enabling new qubit architectures.

title: Engineering Quantum Phases in Two Dimensions via Vacancy-Induced Electronic Reconstruction
summary: Topological phases of matter are commonly understood as emerging either from crystalline symmetry and intrinsic spin-orbit coupling or from disorder-driven electronic renormalization. In realistic materials, however, structural defects naturally combine both ingredients. Here, we demonstrate a general and material-independent mechanism by which atomic vacancies can induce topological phase transitions in two-dimensional semiconductors that are otherwise topologically trivial. Vacancies generate locally ordered dangling-bond states governed by well-defined hopping and spin-orbit interactions, while their spatial distribution and mutual coupling introduce long-range disorder. As vacancy concentration increases, the hybridization of these defect states forms an emergent electronic subspace that undergoes a topological transition. Using a tight-binding framework supported by large-scale density functional theory calculations, we show that this vacancy-induced electronic reconstruction can robustly stabilize quantum spin Hall, quantum anomalous Hall, and Weyl semimetal phases, depending on symmetry breaking and spin polarization. Our results establish vacancies not merely as perturbations, but as active design elements capable of transforming trivial insulators into topological quantum matter, opening realistic routes for defect-engineered topological devices.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that atomic vacancies in 2D semiconductors can serve as active design elements to induce topological phase transitions, transforming trivial insulators into topological quantum matter (quantum spin Hall, quantum anomalous Hall, and Weyl semimetal phases) through vacancy-induced electronic reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Traditional understanding of topological phases relies on either crystalline symmetry with intrinsic spin-orbit coupling or disorder-driven renormalization. The authors aim to explore how structural defects combining both ingredients can create topological phases in otherwise trivial materials.
AI.method: The study employs a tight-binding framework supported by large-scale density functional theory calculations to investigate vacancy-generated dangling-bond states, their hopping and spin-orbit interactions, and the resulting topological transitions as vacancy concentration increases.
AI.result: The hybridization of defect states forms an emergent electronic subspace that undergoes topological transitions, successfully stabilizing quantum spin Hall, quantum anomalous Hall, and Weyl semimetal phases depending on symmetry breaking and spin polarization conditions.
AI.conclusion: Vacancies are not merely perturbations but active design elements capable of transforming trivial insulators into topological quantum matter, providing a material-independent mechanism for defect-engineered topological devices.

title: Field-induced quasi-bound state within the two-magnon continuum of a square-lattice Heisenberg antiferromagnet
comment: Main Manuscript (13 pages, 5 figures) + appended Supplementary Information (SI: 16 pages, 17 figures)
summary: Quantum magnets in two dimensions display strong quantum interaction effects even when magnetically ordered. Using the metal-organic framework material CuF$_2$(D$_2$O)$_2$(pyz), we investigate the field-dependent spin dynamics of the $S = 1/2$ square-lattice Heisenberg antiferromagnet by high-resolution inelastic neutron scattering to applied fields beyond one third of saturation. We discover an anomalously sharp, dispersive ``shadow mode'' residing within the two-magnon continuum, which shadows the dispersion of the transverse one-magnon branches across the Brillouin zone at an offset equal to the Larmor energy. We perform cylinder matrix-product-state (MPS) calculations that reproduce the field-induced spectrum quantitatively and apply a spectrally consistent $1/S$ spin-wave theory to deduce that the ``Larmor-shadow mode'' is a composite two-magnon resonance: a dispersing magnon at wavevector ${\bf Q}$ couples to the uniform Larmor precession at $Γ$, its small intrinsic linewidth indicating a non-perturbative effect of attractive magnon-magnon interactions. Another quantum-fluctuation phenomenon, the zero-field $(π,0)$ anomaly, is lost at increasing fields, which tighten the spectral weight into the one-magnon and Larmor-shadow modes. To our knowledge, these results constitute the first observation of a sharp quasi-bound state embedded in the continuum of a gapless two-dimensional antiferromagnet.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the discovery of a sharp "Larmor-shadow mode" in the quantum spin dynamics of a 2D S=1/2 square-lattice Heisenberg antiferromagnet (CuF2(D2O)2(pyz)), observed via high-resolution inelastic neutron scattering under high magnetic fields exceeding one-third of saturation.
AI.motivation: Two-dimensional quantum magnets exhibit strong quantum fluctuation effects even when magnetically ordered. The researchers aimed to investigate field-dependent spin dynamics and explore quantum phenomena in the two-magnon continuum of a 2D antiferromagnet.
AI.method: The study combined high-resolution inelastic neutron scattering experiments on CuF2(D2O)2(pyz) under applied fields beyond 1/3 of saturation, cylinder matrix-product-state (MPS) calculations for quantitative reproduction of the field-induced spectrum, and a spectrally consistent 1/S spin-wave theory for theoretical interpretation.
AI.result: A sharp dispersive "Larmor-shadow mode" was discovered within the two-magnon continuum, shadowing the transverse one-magnon dispersion across the Brillouin zone at an offset equal to the Larmor energy. This mode was identified as a composite two-magnon resonance - a dispersing magnon coupled to uniform Larmor precession - with a small intrinsic linewidth indicating attractive magnon-magnon interactions. Additionally, the zero-field (π,0) anomaly is lost at increasing fields as spectral weight tightens into the one-magnon and Larmor-shadow modes.
AI.conclusion: These results constitute the first observation of a sharp quasi-bound state embedded in the continuum of a gapless 2D antiferromagnet, demonstrating a non-perturbative effect of attractive magnon-magnon interactions and providing new insights into quantum fluctuation phenomena in 2D quantum magnets.

title: Field-angle dependence of magnetoresistance in UTe2
summary: We theoretically study angle-resolved magnetoresistance under rotated magnetic field in the normal state of a spin-triplet superconductor UTe$_2$. The Wannier model derived from a GGA+$U$ calculation shows quasi-two-dimensional Fermi surfaces with warping in the $k_z$ direction, consistent with quantum oscillation measurements in the high magnetic field regime. Solving the semiclassical Boltzmann equation, we show that the Fermi surface geometry gives rise to oscillations in the magnetoresistance when the field is tilted from the $c$ axis toward the $a$ or $b$ axis. By assuming a band-dependent relaxation time, the calculated angle-resolved magnetoresistance is in good agreement with the recent transport experiment. This is direct evidence for the warped Fermi surface revealed by ordinary intraband transport. It suggests that the hole band with long relaxation time dominates electron transport. The field angle dependence of the Hall resistivity is calculated for further experimental verification.

AI.tldr: Theoretical study of angle-resolved magnetoresistance in the normal state of spin-triplet superconductor UTe2, revealing warped quasi-2D Fermi surfaces through transport measurements.
AI.motivation: To understand the Fermi surface geometry of UTe2 and explain the observed angle-dependent magnetoresistance oscillations in transport experiments.
AI.method: Wannier model derived from GGA+U calculation combined with semiclassical Boltzmann equation solving, assuming band-dependent relaxation time.
AI.result: Good agreement between calculated angle-resolved magnetoresistance and experimental transport data. The hole band with long relaxation time dominates electron transport.
AI.conclusion: Direct evidence for warped Fermi surface in UTe2 through ordinary intraband transport. The hole band dominates conduction. Hall resistivity calculations provided for further experimental verification.

title: Spin crossover in FeO under shock compression
summary: FeO (wüstite), which exhibits complex electronic and structural properties with increasing pressure and temperature, is a key mineralogical phase for understanding deep planetary interiors. However, direct measurements of its spin state at high-pressure and temperature remain challenging in static compression experiments. Here, we employ laser-driven shock compression to extend the FeO principal Hugoniot up to $\sim$900 GPa and perform in situ X-ray diffraction and X-ray emission spectroscopy up to 250 GPa, probing FeO's crystal structure and spin state. We demonstrate a continuous spin crossover of iron in FeO over a broad pressure range, with the high-spin state persisting beyond Earth's core-mantle boundary (CMB) conditions. These observations provide new experimental constraints on iron spin state at extreme conditions essential for geophysical models of (exo)planetary interiors.

AI.tldr: This study investigates FeO (wüstite) under extreme pressure and temperature conditions using laser-driven shock compression, X-ray diffraction, and X-ray emission spectroscopy to understand the spin state of iron at conditions relevant to planetary interiors.
AI.motivation: FeO is a key mineralogical phase for understanding deep planetary interiors, but direct measurements of its spin state at high-pressure and temperature conditions remain challenging in static compression experiments. The spin state of iron affects mineral properties and is essential for geophysical models of (exo)planetary interiors.
AI.method: The researchers employed laser-driven shock compression to extend the FeO principal Hugoniot up to ~900 GPa and performed in situ X-ray diffraction and X-ray emission spectroscopy up to 250 GPa, probing FeO's crystal structure and spin state.
AI.result: The study demonstrates a continuous spin crossover of iron in FeO over a broad pressure range, with the high-spin state persisting beyond Earth's core-mantle boundary (CMB) conditions.
AI.conclusion: These observations provide new experimental constraints on iron spin state at extreme conditions essential for geophysical models of (exo)planetary interiors.

title: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo enhanced by Exact Diagonalization
summary: Strongly correlated fermionic systems are of great interest in condensed matter physics and numerical methods are indispensable tools for their study. However, existing approaches such as exact diagonalization (ED) and stochastic quantum Monte Carlo methods each suffer from fundamental limitations: ED is hindered by exponential scaling in system size, while Monte Carlo methods are plagued by sign problems and long autocorrelation times. These limitations restrict the accessible parameter space and developing algorithms that efficiently alleviate them remains a central challenge in computational physics. In this work, we propose a hybrid algorithm that combines ED and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) to simulate 2D arrays of coupled quantum wires, modeled as interacting fermionic Hubbard chains. We demonstrate how our hybrid implementation of HMC, which we dub H$^2$MC, outperforms either method alone across several key simulation facets. When compared to pure ED, H$^2$MC has a much more favorable computational scaling, which allows us to push simulations to much larger 2D arrays. H$^2$MC also greatly alleviates the sign problem and reduces autocorrelation times when compared to pure HMC formulations utilizing either real or imaginary auxiliary fields. Our formalism demonstrates how complementary strengths of seemingly disparate methods can be leveraged to enable feasible simulations in an extended parameter space.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes H2MC, a hybrid algorithm combining Exact Diagonalization (ED) and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) to simulate 2D arrays of coupled quantum wires modeled as interacting fermionic Hubbard chains. The hybrid approach overcomes limitations of individual methods: ED's exponential scaling and HMC's sign problems/autocorrelation issues.
AI.motivation: Strongly correlated fermionic systems are important in condensed matter physics, but existing numerical methods (ED and quantum Monte Carlo) have fundamental limitations - ED scales exponentially while Monte Carlo suffers from sign problems and long autocorrelation times, restricting accessible parameter space.
AI.method: The authors develop a hybrid algorithm (H2MC) combining ED and HMC to simulate 2D arrays of coupled quantum wires modeled as interacting fermionic Hubbard chains.
AI.result: H2MC outperforms either method alone: it has more favorable scaling than pure ED (enabling larger 2D arrays), and significantly alleviates the sign problem while reducing autocorrelation times compared to pure HMC with real or imaginary auxiliary fields.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach demonstrates how complementary strengths of different methods can be leveraged to enable feasible simulations in an extended parameter space, offering a promising direction for studying strongly correlated fermionic systems.

title: Extended Hubbard model on fractals: d-Wave superconductivity and competing pairing channels
summary: Fractal structures such as the Sierpiński gasket have been predicted to enhance the critical tem- perature of s-wave superconductivity compared to regular crystals while maintaining macroscopic phase coherence of Cooper pairs. Here we extend this analysis to order parameters with non-trivial symmetry by studying the extended Hubbard model with nearest-neighbor attraction on fractal lattices. Using Bogoliubov-de Gennes mean-field theory, we find that the Sierpiński carpet dramat- ically alters the competition between pairing channels: the predominant d-wave superconducting dome at half filling of the square lattice becomes unstable for the carpet, while at high and low fillings extended s-wave pairing gets strongly enhanced. We attribute this to geometric frustration of sign-changing order parameters by the fractal boundary structure. On the triangular Sierpiński gasket, hybrid s+d+id states show critical temperature enhancement comparable to that previously observed for pure s-wave pairing. Our results demonstrate that fractal geometry acts as a selective filter for pairing symmetries, with the compatibility between order parameter structure and lattice topology determining which channels are stabilized or suppressed.

AI.tldr: This study investigates how fractal geometry (Sierpiński carpet and gasket) affects superconductivity with different pairing symmetries (d-wave, extended s-wave, s+d+id) using the extended Hubbard model with nearest-neighbor attraction. Fractal lattices are found to selectively filter pairing symmetries based on compatibility between order parameter structure and lattice topology.
AI.motivation: Extend previous work showing fractal structures enhance s-wave superconductivity to non-trivial pairing symmetries (d-wave, extended s-wave), and understand how fractal geometry influences competition between different pairing channels.
AI.method: Bogoliubov-de Gennes mean-field theory applied to the extended Hubbard model with nearest-neighbor attraction on fractal lattices (Sierpiński carpet and triangular Sierpiński gasket).
AI.result: The Sierpiński carpet destabilizes d-wave pairing at half filling while strongly enhancing extended s-wave pairing at high and low fillings, attributed to geometric frustration of sign-changing order parameters. On triangular Sierpiński gasket, hybrid s+d+id states show critical temperature enhancement comparable to pure s-wave pairing.
AI.conclusion: Fractal geometry acts as a selective filter for pairing symmetries—the compatibility between order parameter structure and lattice topology determines which pairing channels are stabilized or suppressed.

title: Thermodynamic accessibility of Li-Mn-Ti-O cation disordered rock-salt phases
summary: Disordered rock-salt with Li-excess (DRX) cathode phases within the Li-Mn-Ti-O (LMTO) composition space have recently been extensively studied, as they promise to deliver exceptional energy density at low cost in Li-ion batteries. The continued development of LMTO DRX with improved power density and cycling stability requires optimization of the composition and particle size/morphology, which are determined by synthesis conditions such as annealing temperatures and hold times. These challenges motivate our investigation of the phase diagram of the LMTO rock-salt phase space, with a focus on understanding the stability of DRX by quantifying the order-disorder transition temperature ($T_\text{disord}$) as a function of composition. We harness first-principles calculations and X-ray diffraction experiments to establish the LMTO phase diagram, which lies within the LiMnO$_2$ -- Li$_2$MnO$_3$ -- Li$_2$TiO$_3$ pseudo-ternary. Our calculations predict that the LMTO phase diagram at elevated temperature ($700 - 1300$ C) is composed of three phases: DRX, orthorhombic LiMnO$_2$, and layered Li$_2$Mn$_\text{1-y}$Ti$_\text{y}$O$_3$ ($0 < \text{y} < 1$). $T_\text{disord}$ decreases significantly as off-stoichiometry is introduced to the end-point compositions, resulting in a eutectoid phase diagram. Importantly, a significant range of LMTO compositions containing small to moderate fractions of Li-excess and Ti doping (relative to LiMnO$_2$) have $T_\text{disord}$ spanning $700 - 900$ C. These temperatures are substantially lower than conventional DRX synthesis temperatures ($\geq 1000$ C), suggesting the promise of decreasing synthesis temperatures for specific DRX compositions. The compositions containing moderate to high fractions of Mn$^{4+}$ instead have much greater $T_\text{disord}$ and phase separation to layered Li$_2$MnO$_3$ becomes highly favored.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the phase diagram of Li-Mn-Ti-O (LMTO) disordered rock-salt (DRX) cathode materials for Li-ion batteries. Using first-principles calculations and X-ray diffraction experiments, the researchers mapped the phase boundaries between DRX, orthorhombic LiMnO2, and layered Li2Mn1-yTiyO3 phases. The study reveals that order-disorder transition temperature (T_disord) decreases significantly with off-stoichiometry, and certain DRX compositions can be synthesized at lower temperatures (700-900°C) than conventional methods (≥1000°C).
AI.motivation: The development of LMTO DRX cathodes promises exceptional energy density at low cost for Li-ion batteries. However, improving power density and cycling stability requires optimization of composition and particle morphology, which are determined by synthesis conditions. This motivated investigation of the LMTO phase diagram to understand DRX stability and quantify the order-disorder transition temperature as a function of composition.
AI.method: The researchers used first-principles calculations combined with X-ray diffraction experiments to establish the LMTO phase diagram within the LiMnO2 -- Li2MnO3 -- Li2TiO3 pseudo-ternary system. They studied compositions at elevated temperatures (700-1300°C).
AI.result: The LMTO phase diagram at elevated temperature consists of three phases: DRX, orthorhombic LiMnO2, and layered Li2Mn1-yTiyO3. T_disord decreases significantly with off-stoichiometry, forming a eutectoid phase diagram. Compositions with small to moderate Li-excess and Ti doping have T_disord spanning 700-900°C, substantially lower than conventional DRX synthesis temperatures (≥1000°C). Compositions with high Mn4+ fractions have much greater T_disord and tend toward phase separation to layered Li2MnO3.
AI.conclusion: A significant range of LMTO DRX compositions can be synthesized at lower temperatures (700-900°C) than conventional methods, suggesting promise for reducing synthesis costs. However, compositions with high Mn4+ content show greater T_disord and favor phase separation to layered Li2MnO3, which may limit their practical application.

title: Site-selective renormalization and competing magnetic instabilities in paramagnet Y$_{3}$Cu$_{2}$Sb$_{3}$O$_{14}$
summary: Quantum spin liquids (QSLs) are exotic phases of matter characterized by long-range entanglement and the absence of magnetic order even at zero temperature. Here, we present a comprehensive theoretical study of the frustrated magnet Y$_{3}$Cu$_{2}$Sb$_{3}$O$_{14}$ to elucidate its electronic and magnetic properties. We uncover two inequivalent Cu sites with fundamentally distinct oxygen coordination, i.e. octahedral CuO$_6$ and axially compressed CuO$_8$, that give rise to completely opposite crystal-field splittings. This inversion places the unpaired hole in the $d_{z^2}$ orbital at the Cu-2 site, while Cu-1 maintains conventional $d_{x^2-y^2}/d_{xy}$ character, producing narrow bands at commensurate filling. Dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) calculation reveals a selective band-renormalization of orbitals from the two Cu ions with Cu-1 undergoing a Mott transition while Cu-2 remaining metallic under electronic correlations. Crucially, FLEX calculations demonstrate that multiple magnetic instabilities compete with nearly equal strength: the spin susceptibility lacks dominant peaks, and the leading eigenvalues approach unity simultaneously across all wavevectors with increasing interaction. This competitive interplay, originating from the distinct local environments and geometric frustration on the triangular lattice, agrees well with the absence of long-range magnetic order in experiment. Our results suggest Y$_{3}$Cu$_{2}$Sb$_{3}$O$_{14}$ as a promising QSL candidate where the unique combination of disparate crystal-field environments, strong correlations, and competing exchange interactions conspire to stabilize an exotic quantum ground state.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates Y3Cu2Sb3O14 as a promising quantum spin liquid (QSL) candidate, where two inequivalent Cu sites with opposite crystal-field splittings create competing magnetic instabilities that prevent long-range magnetic order at zero temperature.
AI.motivation: To understand the exotic quantum spin liquid state in Y3Cu2Sb3O14 by elucidating its electronic and magnetic properties, particularly the origin of the absence of magnetic order despite strong frustration.
AI.method: Combined theoretical approach using: (1) Crystal-field analysis to understand site-specific orbital configurations; (2) Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) to calculate band renormalization and correlation effects; (3) FLEX (fluctuation-exchange) calculations to map out magnetic susceptibility and competing instabilities.
AI.result: Two inequivalent Cu sites (Cu-1 octahedral and Cu-2 axially compressed) exhibit opposite crystal-field splittings. Cu-2 has unoccupied dz2 orbital while Cu-1 maintains conventional dx2-y2/xy character. DMFT reveals selective band renormalization: Cu-1 undergoes Mott transition while Cu-2 remains metallic. FLEX shows competing magnetic instabilities with nearly equal strength across all wavevectors, lacking dominant peaks in spin susceptibility.
AI.conclusion: Y3Cu2Sb3O14 is a promising QSL candidate where disparate crystal-field environments, strong correlations, and competing exchange interactions on the triangular lattice combine to stabilize an exotic quantum ground state without long-range magnetic order.

title: ${H}$-linear magnetoresistance in the ${T^2}$ resistivity regime of overdoped infinite-layer nickelate La$_{1-x}$Sr$_{x}$NiO$_2$
summary: We report a systematic magnetotransport study on high-crystallinity La$_{1-x}$Sr$_{x}$NiO$_2$ (LSNO) thin films with $x=0.20-0.24$. By conducting pulsed-field transport experiment up to 62 T, we reveal two salient features of the normal-state transport in overdoped LSNO thin films: (1) the magnetoresistance does not follow the Kohler's rule but exhibits a $H$-linear behavior in the high $H/T$ limit and (2) the normal-state $ρ(T)$ below 30 K consistently follows a $T^2$ behavior across the overdoped regime. Our results demonstrate a coexistence of $H$-linear magnetoresistance and $T^2$ resistivity in a model unconventional superconductor and provide new information on the transport characteristics of the normal ground state that host superconductivity in infinite-layer nickelates.

AI.tldr: Systematic magnetotransport study on overdoped La1-xSrxNiO2 thin films (x=0.20-0.24) reveals unusual transport properties: H-linear magnetoresistance violating Kohler's rule and T^2 resistivity below 30 K, suggesting non-Fermi liquid behavior in these unconventional superconductors.
AI.motivation: Investigate the normal-state transport properties of overdoped infinite-layer nickelates to understand the underlying physics of superconductivity in these materials.
AI.method: Pulsed-field magnetotransport measurements up to 62 T on high-crystallinity La1-xSrxNiO2 thin films.
AI.result: Two key findings: (1) Magnetoresistance violates Kohler's rule and exhibits H-linear behavior in high H/T limit; (2) Normal-state resistivity ρ(T) follows T^2 dependence below 30 K consistently across the overdoped regime (x=0.20-0.24).
AI.conclusion: The coexistence of H-linear magnetoresistance and T^2 resistivity provides new insights into the normal ground state of infinite-layer nickelates and suggests non-Fermi liquid behavior in these unconventional superconductors.

title: Symmetry-Driven Electrical Switching of Anisotropic Skyrmion Hall Effect in Altermagnets
summary: Controlling the skyrmion Hall effect (SkHE) is pivotal for developing topological spintronics but typically relies on magnetic field reversal. Here, we demonstrate a general strategy for the purely electrical switching of the SkHE in two-dimensional altermagnets. Through symmetry and model analysis, we reveal that the intrinsic altermagnetic symmetry imposes sublattice-dependent anisotropic exchange and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions. These interactions induce a highly anisotropic SkHE, where the transverse velocity is strictly dictated by the current direction relative to the crystal axes. Crucially, we show that an external electric field can strongly modulate these interaction parameters by inversing the altermagnetic symmetry, allowing for the reversible inversion of the anisotropic SkHE. Using first-principles and atomistic spin model simulations, this mechanism is further demonstrated in monolayer CaMnSn. Our study establishes a unique strategy for realizing precise, electrically tunable skyrmion transport without magnetic fields.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates a strategy for purely electrical control of skyrmion Hall effect (SkHE) in 2D altermagnets by using external electric fields to modulate anisotropic exchange and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions, enabling reversible inversion of SkHE without magnetic fields.
AI.motivation: Controlling the skyrmion Hall effect currently relies on magnetic field reversal, which limits applications in topological spintronics. There is a need for electrical control methods that enable precise, tunable skyrmion transport.
AI.method: The researchers used symmetry analysis, model analysis, first-principles calculations, and atomistic spin model simulations to investigate the mechanism in monolayer CaMnSn.
AI.result: They discovered that altermagnetic symmetry induces sublattice-dependent anisotropic exchange and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions, creating a highly anisotropic SkHE where transverse velocity depends on current direction relative to crystal axes. External electric fields can invert the altermagnetic symmetry, enabling reversible modulation of these interactions and the SkHE.
AI.conclusion: This study establishes a unique strategy for realizing precise, electrically tunable skyrmion transport without magnetic fields, with potential applications in topological spintronics devices.

title: Emergent superconformal symmetry in the phase diagram of a 1D $\mathbb{Z}_{2}$ lattice gauge theory
summary: We investigate the phase diagram and critical properties of a one-dimensional $\mathbb{Z}_{2}$ lattice gauge theory describing an orthogonal metal, where spinless fermions and Ising spins are minimally coupled to a deconfined $\mathbb{Z}_{2}$ gauge field. Working at half-filling of fermions, we derive an exact gauge-invariant formulation that maps the model onto decoupled XXZ and transverse-field Ising chains. This mapping enables a controlled low-energy field-theory description in terms of a perturbed Luttinger liquid and Ising conformal field theories. Combining analytical arguments with numerical simulations, we determine the full phase diagram and identify various critical and multi-critical regimes. Along a specific multi-critical line, where the fermionic and bosonic velocities coincide, we find strong evidence for an emergent superconformal symmetry. Our results establish a minimal lattice realization of emergent superconformal criticalities in a gauge-matter system and provide a route toward its exploration in quantum simulators.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates a 1D Z2 lattice gauge theory modeling an orthogonal metal with spinless fermions and Ising spins coupled to a Z2 gauge field. By mapping the model to decoupled XXZ and transverse-field Ising chains, the authors determine the full phase diagram and discover emergent superconformal symmetry at a multi-critical point where fermionic and bosonic velocities coincide.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand critical properties and phase transitions in gauge-matter systems, particularly to find a minimal lattice realization of emergent superconformal criticality, which is theoretically significant and potentially explorable in quantum simulators.
AI.method: The authors use exact gauge-invariant mapping to decoupled XXZ and transverse-field Ising chains, combined with analytical field-theory descriptions (perturbed Luttinger liquid and Ising CFT) and numerical simulations.
AI.result: The paper establishes the full phase diagram with various critical and multi-critical regimes, and provides strong evidence for emergent superconformal symmetry along a specific multi-critical line where fermionic and bosonic velocities coincide.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the minimal lattice realization of emergent superconformal criticalities in a gauge-matter system and opens a pathway for exploration in quantum simulators.

title: Superconducting Lanthanum Nickel Oxides with Bilayered and Trilayered Crystal Structures
comment: The revised version of this paper has been published in J. Phys. Condens. Matter 38 (2026) 073002. DOI: 10.1088/1361-648X/ae4155
summary:
In 2023, superconductivity in La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ was discovered under high pressures above approximately 14 GPa. In addition to its high transition temperature ($T_{\mathrm{c}} \simeq 80$ K), the structural resemblance to high-$T_{\mathrm{c}}$ cuprates has strongly stimulated research, soon followed by the discovery of superconductivity in La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$. These compounds belong to the Ruddlesden--Popper phases, comprising double- and triple-layered NiO$_2$ square lattices separated by LaO rock-salt slabs.
  Research on these systems has rapidly developed along three major directions, as in other prominent families of superconductors such as the cuprates and iron arsenides: expanding the chemical variety of compounds, enhancing $T_{\mathrm{c}}$ through elemental substitution, and elucidating the superconducting mechanism. These challenges, being closely interconnected, continue to drive the field. The clarification of the pairing mechanism encounters a particular difficulty, since the key experiments must be performed under high pressures. This situation highlights the significance of developing nickel oxides that exhibit superconductivity at much lower pressures, ideally at ambient pressure, which would in turn broaden the scope of chemical tuning and detailed physical characterization.
  In this context, it is timely and meaningful to summarize the present state of knowledge. Here, we emphasize sample synthesis and characterization, which are already well established and often decisive for progress in unconventional superconductors, while providing a brief overview of the currently available electronic properties.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews the rapidly developing field of nickel oxide superconductivity, focusing on La3Ni2O7 (Tc ~80 K, discovered 2023 under >14 GPa) and La4Ni3O10. These Ruddlesden-Popper phase compounds show structural similarities to high-Tc cuprates, driving research on chemical variety expansion, Tc enhancement, and pairing mechanism elucidation. The key challenge is developing superconductors that operate at lower pressures for broader characterization.
AI.motivation: The discovery of superconductivity in La3Ni2O7 at ~80 K under high pressure in 2023 generated significant interest due to its structural resemblance to high-Tc cuprates. The field quickly expanded with discovery in La4Ni3O10. However, performing key experiments under high pressure presents major difficulties, making it crucial to develop nickel oxides that superconduct at lower pressures or ambient conditions.
AI.method: This is a review paper summarizing the present state of knowledge in nickel oxide superconductors. The authors emphasize sample synthesis and characterization methods while providing an overview of available electronic properties data.
AI.result: The research on these systems has developed along three major directions: (1) expanding chemical variety of compounds, (2) enhancing Tc through elemental substitution, and (3) elucidating the superconducting mechanism. These interconnected challenges continue to drive the field forward.
AI.conclusion: Developing nickel oxides that exhibit superconductivity at much lower pressures, ideally ambient pressure, is crucial for broadening chemical tuning options and enabling detailed physical characterization. This will help advance understanding of the pairing mechanism in these unconventional superconductors.

title: Single-pair charge-2 Weyl-Dirac composite semimetals
summary: The Nielsen--Ninomiya theorem requires that the total topological chiral charges in a crystal vanish, a constraint typically satisfied by identical nodes like Weyl--Weyl pairs. Whether a minimal heterogeneous configuration -- comprising a single Weyl point (WP) and a single Dirac point (DP) -- can exist in an electronic system has remained unresolved. Here, by systematically classifying all 1651 magnetic space groups (MSGs), we reveal that only 14 MSGs without spin-orbit coupling (SOC) and 10 MSGs with SOC are compatible with this exotic state. Furthermore, for nonmagnetic crystals, this configuration is uniquely realized in the spinless limit of chiral space groups 92 and 96. Guided by this principle, we predict an ideal realization in chiral three-dimensional boron allotropes (SDHBN-B$_{28}$ enantiomers). First-principles calculations unveil a $|C|=2$ WP at the $Γ$ point and a $|C|=2$ DP at the $A$ point, which constitute the only fermions near the Fermi level within a large $2$ eV energy window. Strikingly, the structural chirality rigidly dictates the sign of the topological charges, yielding two ultralong Fermi arcs spanning the surface Brillouin zone. Our work provides a complete crystallographic classification and a definitive material platform for exploring minimal heterogeneous chiral fermions.

AI.tldr: The paper resolves a long-standing question in topological physics: whether a minimal heterogeneous configuration containing a single Weyl point (WP) and a single Dirac point (DP) can exist in crystals. Through exhaustive classification of all 1651 magnetic space groups, the authors identify the exact crystallographic constraints and predict the first real material realization in chiral boron allotropes (SDHBN-B28).
AI.motivation: The Nielsen-Ninomiya theorem mandates that total topological chiral charges in a crystal must vanish, typically satisfied by paired nodes like Weyl-Weyl pairs. The question of whether a single Weyl point can coexist with a single Dirac point—the minimal heterogeneous configuration—has remained unresolved despite its fundamental importance for topological physics and material discovery.
AI.method: The authors performed a systematic classification of all 1651 magnetic space groups (MSGs), analyzing which groups can support a WP-DP pair without violating the Nielsen-Ninomiya theorem. They considered both cases with and without spin-orbit coupling (SOC). For the predicted material candidate, first-principles calculations were conducted to verify the topological fermion configurations.
AI.result: Only 14 MSGs without SOC and 10 MSGs with SOC are compatible with the WP-DP configuration. For nonmagnetic crystals, this exotic state is uniquely realized in chiral space groups 92 and 96 in the spinless limit. The ideal realization was predicted in chiral 3D boron allotropes (SDHBN-B28 enantiomers), featuring a |C|=2 Weyl point at the Γ point and a |C|=2 Dirac point at the A point—the only fermions near the Fermi level within a 2 eV energy window. Two ultralong Fermi arcs span the surface Brillouin zone, with their sign rigidly dictated by structural chirality.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a complete crystallographic classification for minimal heterogeneous chiral fermions and identifies SDHBN-B28 as the definitive material platform for exploring this exotic topological state. The findings bridge the gap between abstract topological constraints and tangible material realizations, opening new avenues for studying chiral fermion physics.

title: Magnetism, electronic transport, and disorder in strongly correlated systems
comment: PhD Thesis, Universidad de Buenos Aires, 2026
summary:
This thesis investigates the magnetic, spectral, and transport properties of strongly correlated electronic systems, with a primary focus on the Hubbard model and its extensions relevant for real materials. Within the dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) framework, different regimes of interaction strength, temperature, doping, and magnetic field are explored, highlighting the central role of local electronic correlations in shaping spectral reconstruction and nontrivial transport responses.
