  For the antiferromagnetic Hubbard model under a Zeeman field, magnetoresistance and local metamagnetism are characterized, revealing the coexistence of distinct energy scales associated with charge and spin degrees of freedom. A minimal, purely correlation-driven mechanism for generating spin-polarized charge transport in structurally conventional collinear antiferromagnets is identified, controlled by the simultaneous breaking of particle--hole symmetry and antiferromagnetic sublattice equivalence.
  Finally, these concepts are applied to correlated materials with strong spin--orbit coupling, such as Sr$_2$IrO$_4$ and Sr$_3$Ir$_2$O$_7$, and to nanoparticle solids dominated by Coulomb blockade and disorder. The results show how ideas developed in correlated lattice models provide a unified interpretation of metal--insulator transitions and spectral reconstruction in complex systems.

AI.tldr: This thesis explores strongly correlated electronic systems using dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT), focusing on the Hubbard model. It investigates magnetic, spectral, and transport properties under various conditions (interaction strength, temperature, doping, magnetic field), revealing mechanisms for spin-polarized charge transport in antiferromagnets and applying findings to iridates and nanoparticle solids.
AI.motivation: Understanding strongly correlated electronic systems remains a central challenge in condensed matter physics. The Hubbard model serves as a canonical framework, but its application to real materials with complex interactions (spin-orbit coupling, disorder, Coulomb blockade) requires deeper investigation of how local electronic correlations shape spectral and transport properties.
AI.method: Dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) is employed to study the Hubbard model and its extensions across different parameter regimes: interaction strength, temperature, doping, and magnetic field. The approach examines antiferromagnetic systems under Zeeman fields and applies concepts to materials with strong spin-orbit coupling (Sr₂IrO₄, Sr₃Ir₂O₇) and disordered nanoparticle solids.
AI.result: Key findings include: (1) magnetoresistance and local metamagnetism in the antiferromagnetic Hubbard model under Zeeman field, revealing coexisting energy scales for charge and spin; (2) identification of a correlation-driven mechanism for spin-polarized charge transport in conventional collinear antiferromagnets, requiring simultaneous breaking of particle-hole symmetry and antiferromagnetic sublattice equivalence; (3) successful application to correlated materials with strong spin-orbit coupling and disordered systems, providing unified interpretation of metal-insulator transitions and spectral reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: The thesis demonstrates that ideas developed from correlated lattice models offer a unified framework for understanding metal-insulator transitions and spectral reconstruction across diverse systems—from bulk correlated materials like iridates to disordered nanoparticle solids. The correlation-driven mechanism for spin-polarized transport in conventional antiferromagnets represents a significant finding with potential implications for spintronic applications.

title: Electron-Hole Scattering Dichotomy and Anisotropic Warping in Quasi-Two-Dimensional Fermi Surfaces of UTe2
summary: We present a combined experimental and theoretical study of the detailed Fermi-surface (FS) geometry of UTe2, a heavy-fermion superconductor that has recently attracted considerable attention as a promising candidate for spin-triplet pairing. Using angle-dependent magnetoresistance oscillations, a bulk- and low-energy-sensitive transport probe for quasi-two-dimensional (Q2D) electronic structures, we directly determine the in-plane FS geometry. We found that the Q2D FS exhibits a rectangular cross-sectional shape with strongly anisotropic warping, originating from the hybridization of two orthogonal quasi-one-dimensional bands. Through a quantitative comparison between experiment and theoretical calculations, we further reveal a large electron-hole scattering dichotomy: the quasiparticle lifetime on the electron FS is substantially shorter than that on the hole FS. This dichotomy is naturally explained by anisotropic, low-dimensional antiferromagnetic fluctuations, which selectively enhance scattering on the electron FS. This suggests a dominant role of the electron pockets for the emergence of superconductivity. Our results clarify a direct relation between FS geometry, magnetic fluctuations, and momentum-dependent quasiparticle lifetimes, and thus providing a crucial basis for the microscopic understanding of pairing mechanism, and impose stringent constraints on the gap symmetry of spin-triplet superconductivity in UTe2.

AI.tldr: Study of Fermi-surface geometry in UTe2 using angle-dependent magnetoresistance, revealing rectangular cross-sectional shape with anisotropic warping from hybridization of two quasi-one-dimensional bands, and discovering large electron-hole scattering dichotomy with implications for spin-triplet superconductivity.
AI.motivation: Understanding the Fermi-surface geometry and its relationship to magnetic fluctuations and quasiparticle lifetimes is crucial for determining the pairing mechanism of spin-triplet superconductivity in UTe2.
AI.method: Combined experimental and theoretical approach using angle-dependent magnetoresistance oscillations (a bulk- and low-energy-sensitive transport probe for quasi-2D electronic structures) to directly determine in-plane FS geometry, with quantitative comparison to theoretical calculations.
AI.result: The Q2D FS exhibits a rectangular cross-sectional shape with strongly anisotropic warping from hybridization of two orthogonal quasi-one-dimensional bands. A large electron-hole scattering dichotomy was found: quasiparticle lifetime on electron FS is substantially shorter than on hole FS, explained by anisotropic, low-dimensional antiferromagnetic fluctuations that selectively enhance scattering on electron FS.
AI.conclusion: Results clarify direct relation between FS geometry, magnetic fluctuations, and momentum-dependent quasiparticle lifetimes, providing crucial basis for understanding pairing mechanism and imposing stringent constraints on gap symmetry of spin-triplet superconductivity in UTe2. Suggests dominant role of electron pockets for superconductivity emergence.

title: GPUMDkit: A User-Friendly Toolkit for GPUMD and NEP
summary: Machine-learned interatomic potentials have revolutionized molecular dynamics simulations by providing quantum-mechanical accuracy at empirical-potential speeds. The graphics processing unit molecular dynamics (GPUMD) package, featuring the highly efficient neuroevolution potential (NEP) framework, has emerged as a powerful tool in this domain. However, the complexity of force field development, active learning, and trajectory post-processing often requires extensive manual scripting, imposing a steep learning curve on new users. To address this, we present GPUMDkit, a comprehensive and user-friendly toolkit that streamlines the entire simulation workflow for GPUMD and NEP. GPUMDkit integrates a suite of essential functionalities, including format conversion, structure sampling, property calculation, and data visualization, accessible through both interactive and command-line interfaces. Its modular, extensible architecture ensures accessibility for users of all experience levels while allowing seamless integration of new features. By automating complex tasks and enhancing productivity, GPUMDkit substantially lowers the barrier to using GPUMD and NEP programs. This article describes the program architecture and demonstrates its capabilities through practical applications.

title: Toward bootstrapping tensor-network contractions
summary: Accurate contraction of tensor networks beyond one dimension is essential in various fields including quantum many-body physics. Existing approaches typically rely on approximate contraction schemes and do not provide certified error bars. We introduce a numerical bootstrap framework which casts the problem of tensor-network contractions into a convex optimization problem, thereby yielding certified lower and upper bounds on expectation values of physical observables. As a proof-of-principle, we construct such constraints explicitly for translationally invariant matrix product states and demonstrate that, assuming a canonical form, second-order-cone relaxation can provide tight bounds on the contraction result. We further demonstrate that when the requirement on canonical form is lifted, a more general semidefinite-programming approach could yield similar tight bounds at higher but still polynomial computational cost. Our work suggests numerical bootstrap could be a possible way forward for the rigorous contractions of tensor networks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a numerical bootstrap framework for tensor network contractions that provides certified error bounds by formulating the problem as convex optimization. The approach uses second-order-cone and semidefinite-programming relaxations to obtain tight bounds on physical observables in translationally invariant matrix product states.
AI.motivation: Existing tensor network contraction methods in dimensions >1 are approximate and lack certified error bars, which is a significant limitation for reliable quantum many-body physics calculations.
AI.method: The authors convert tensor network contraction into a convex optimization problem, specifically using second-order-cone relaxation (assuming canonical form) and more general semidefinite-programming approaches (without canonical form requirement).
AI.result: The method provides certified lower and upper bounds on expectation values of physical observables, with tight bounds achieved at polynomial computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Numerical bootstrap represents a promising pathway for rigorous tensor network contractions with certified error estimates in quantum many-body systems.

title: Reaching Quantum Critical Point by Adding Non-magnetic Disorder in Single Crystals of Superconductor $(\text{Ca}_x\text{Sr}_{1-x})_3\text{Rh}_4\text{Sn}_{13}$
summary: The Remeika series superconductor, $(\text{Ca}_x\text{Sr}_{1-x})_3\text{Rh}_4\text{Sn}_{13}$, shows a rare nonmagnetic quantum critical point (QCP) associated with the continuous charge-density wave (CDW) and structural transition under the ``dome'' of superconductivity achieved by tuning composition and applying pressure. Here we use a nonmagnetic point-like disorder induced by 2.5 MeV electron irradiation to suppress the CDW and drive the system to and even beyond the QCP. This conclusion is based on a clear evolution of temperature-dependent resistivity, $ρ\left(T\right)$, from the Fermi liquid to the non-Fermi liquid regime with increasing amount of disorder. Starting on the CDW side, below the suggested QCP concentration of $x_c=0.9$, added disorder resulted in a progressively larger linear term and a reduced quadratic term in $ρ\left(T\right)$. Nearly perfect $T-$linear dependence is observed at the dose at which long-range CDW order is suppressed to $T=$0, consistent with the expectations. We refine the QCP location in this system and place it in the interval between $x=$0.75 and 0.85. Our results strongly support the concept that the disorder can tune the system to the quantum critical regime and even beyond. It follows from the argument by Imry and Ma that any ordered phase is unstable toward quenched disorder. Introduced in a controlled way, this disorder becomes a novel non-thermal tuning parameter likely applicable to a variety of different systems.

AI.tldr: The Remeika series superconductor (Ca_xSr_{1-x})_3Rh_4Sn_{13} exhibits a rare nonmagnetic quantum critical point (QCP) associated with a charge-density wave (CDW) transition. Using 2.5 MeV electron irradiation to introduce nonmagnetic point-like disorder, researchers suppressed the CDW and tuned the system to and beyond the QCP. The evolution from Fermi liquid to non-Fermi liquid behavior was observed through resistivity measurements, with T-linear dependence emerging at the critical dose where long-range CDW order is suppressed. The QCP was refined to between x=0.75 and 0.85.
AI.motivation: Understanding quantum criticality in superconductors and exploring disorder as a non-thermal tuning parameter to study quantum phase transitions. The goal was to suppress CDW order and access the quantum critical regime through controlled disorder introduction.
AI.method: 2.5 MeV electron irradiation was used to introduce nonmagnetic point-like disorder in (Ca_xSr_{1-x})_3Rh_4Sn_{13} single crystals. Temperature-dependent resistivity ρ(T) measurements were performed to track the evolution from Fermi liquid to non-Fermi liquid behavior as disorder increased.
AI.result: With increasing disorder, the resistivity showed progressive reduction of quadratic term and increase of linear T term. Nearly perfect T-linear dependence was observed at the dose where long-range CDW order is suppressed to T=0. The QCP was refined to the interval between x=0.75 and 0.85.
AI.conclusion: Disorder can serve as a novel non-thermal tuning parameter to access quantum critical regimes. The Imry-Ma argument suggests any ordered phase is unstable toward quenched disorder. This controlled disorder approach is likely applicable to various other systems exhibiting quantum criticality.

title: Direct observation of ultrafast amorphous-amorphous transitions indicated by bond stretching and angle bending in phase-change material GeTe
summary: The intrinsic nature of glass states and glass transitions at the atomic scale remain a fundamental open question in condensed-matter physics and materials science. By combining femtosecond electron diffraction with time-dependent density-functional theory molecular dynamics simulations, we directly observe ultrafast amorphous-amorphous transitions in amorphous GeTe, manifested as rapid Ge-Te (Ge) bond stretching within 0.2 ps and subsequent angle bending of the Ge-Te (Ge)-Ge motif on a 0.5-2 ps timescale. Critically, the ultrafast bond stretching is accompanied by localized oscillation modes with the frequency of 3.10 THz, unambiguously signaling the local Peierls-like bonding structure and the flexibility of these polarized bonds. These ultrafast collective atomic motions provide a direct structural origin for the boson peak and pay the way for systematic optimization of relaxation and crystallization kinetics.

AI.tldr: This study directly observes ultrafast amorphous-amorphous transitions in amorphous GeTe using femtosecond electron diffraction and TD-DFT MD simulations, revealing bond stretching within 0.2 ps and angle bending on 0.5-2 ps timescales, accompanied by 3.10 THz oscillation modes that provide structural origin for the boson peak.
AI.motivation: The fundamental nature of glass states and glass transitions at the atomic scale remains an open question in condensed-matter physics and materials science, requiring direct observation of atomic-scale dynamics.
AI.method: Femtosecond electron diffraction combined with time-dependent density-functional theory (TD-DFT) molecular dynamics simulations were used to directly observe ultrafast amorphous-amorphous transitions in amorphous GeTe.
AI.result: Rapid Ge-Te bond stretching occurs within 0.2 ps, followed by angle bending of Ge-Te-Ge motif on 0.5-2 ps timescale. The ultrafast bond stretching is accompanied by localized oscillation modes at 3.10 THz, signaling local Peierls-like bonding structure and flexibility of polarized bonds.
AI.conclusion: These ultrafast collective atomic motions provide a direct structural origin for the boson peak and offer a pathway for systematic optimization of relaxation and crystallization kinetics in amorphous materials.

title: Non-Fermi-liquid behaviour of electrons coupled to gauge phonons
summary: We identify overdamped gauge phonons as a new microscopic route to non-Fermi-liquid behaviour in Dirac materials. These phonons couple to electronic currents rather than densities, thereby realising a lattice analogue of transverse gauge-field mechanisms without requiring proximity to a quantum critical point. By computing the electronic self-energy with a phonon propagator dressed by electron-phonon interactions, we show that the low-energy behaviour is controlled by the orbital susceptibility chi and a dimensionless damping parameter alpha. In the overdamped regime, alpha >> 1, quasiparticles display strong deviations from Fermi-liquid theory. For chi > 0, Fermi-liquid behaviour persists only in a parametrically narrow infrared window before crossing over to non-Fermi-liquid scaling. For chi < 0, the Fermi-liquid regime is replaced by marginal-Fermi-liquid behaviour at the lowest energies, followed by a crossover to non-Fermi-liquid scaling. These results establish strain- induced gauge phonons as a promising source of anomalous metallic behaviour in systems such as twisted bilayer graphene.

AI.tldr: The study identifies overdamped gauge phonons as a novel microscopic mechanism enabling non-Fermi-liquid behavior in Dirac materials without requiring proximity to a quantum critical point. These phonons couple to electronic currents rather than densities, acting as lattice analogues of transverse gauge fields.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to find new routes to non-Fermi-liquid behavior in Dirac materials (such as twisted bilayer graphene) that don't rely on proximity to quantum critical points. The goal is to identify alternative microscopic mechanisms for anomalous metallic behavior.
AI.method: The researchers computed the electronic self-energy using a phonon propagator dressed by electron-phonon interactions, analyzing the low-energy behavior controlled by orbital susceptibility (chi) and a dimensionless damping parameter (alpha).
AI.result: In the overdamped regime (alpha >> 1), quasiparticles show strong deviations from Fermi-liquid theory. For chi > 0, Fermi-liquid behavior persists only in a narrow infrared window before crossing to non-Fermi-liquid scaling. For chi < 0, marginal-Fermi-liquid behavior appears at the lowest energies, followed by crossover to non-Fermi-liquid scaling.
AI.conclusion: Strain-induced gauge phonons represent a promising source of anomalous metallic behavior in Dirac material systems like twisted bilayer graphene, offering a viable alternative to quantum critical point-based explanations for non-Fermi-liquid phenomena.

title: Pressure-induced Superconductivity in AgSbTe2
summary: AgSbTe2 is a well-known thermoelectric material with a high Seebeck coefficient and intrinsically low thermal conductivity, but its behavior under pressure remains largely unexplored. Here we report a systematic investigation of the structural, electronic, and transport properties of non-stoichiometric AgSbTe2 under high pressure. At ambient pressure, the material can be described as having a cubic crystal structure that remains stable up to 21.7 GPa beyond which it loses long-range structural order, while its crystal system fully recovers upon decompression. Remarkably, superconductivity emerges at a very low pressure of 0.38 GPa with an onset superconducting critical temperature (Tc) of 3.2 K. Tc increases with increasing pressure, reaching 6.9 K at 31.9 GPa, and peaks at 7.4 K during decompression. Magnetic-field-dependent transport measurements and electronic structure calculations reveal an evolution of the superconducting state driven by an enhanced electronic density of states at the Fermi level under compression. Our findings uncover pressure-induced superconductivity in AgSbTe2 and demonstrate that pressure can effectively tune the electronic ground state of thermoelectric materials, extending their functionality beyond thermoelectric energy conversion.

title: Hydrogen uptake and hydride formation in Al$_x$CoCrFeNi high-entropy alloys: First-principles, universal-potential, and experimental study
summary: Hydrogen uptake in complex multicomponent alloys, including high-entropy alloys (HEAs), governs both hydrogen storage capacity and resistance to hydrogen-induced degradation. We combine high-pressure experiments, density-functional theory (DFT), and a GRACE universal interatomic potential to investigate hydrogen absorption in Al$_{0.3}$CoCrFeNi and Al$_3$CoCrFeNi HEAs. In H$_2$ as a pressure-transmitting medium, the FCC Al$_{0.3}$CoCrFeNi alloy forms hydrides at ambient temperature above 3 GPa, whereas the Al-rich B2 Al$_3$CoCrFeNi alloy shows no evidence of hydride formation even upon heating at pressures up to 50 GPa. Experiments and calculations show that aluminum suppresses hydrogen uptake by increasing solution energies and destabilizing interstitial sites. The universal potential, employed in the calculations and pretrained on large DFT databases, closely reproduces DFT energetics and demonstrates transferability from the dilute limit to the hydride-forming regime. Simulations further disentangle the roles of local ordering, volume changes, composition, and crystal structure. Overall, our results indicate that hydrogen solubility in Al-containing HEAs is governed primarily by composition, with Al-driven B2 ordering as a strong secondary effect.

AI.tldr: This study investigates hydrogen absorption in Al-containing high-entropy alloys (Al₀.₃CoCrFeNi and Al₃CoCrFeNi) using high-pressure experiments, DFT calculations, and machine learning potentials. The FCC alloy forms hydrides above 3 GPa, while the B2 alloy shows no hydride formation even at 50 GPa. Aluminum suppresses hydrogen uptake by increasing solution energies and destabilizing interstitial sites.
AI.motivation: Understanding hydrogen uptake in high-entropy alloys is critical for applications involving hydrogen storage and resistance to hydrogen-induced degradation. The role of aluminum composition and B2 ordering in governing hydrogen solubility in HEAs remained unclear, motivating this combined experimental and computational investigation.
AI.method: The researchers combined high-pressure hydrogen experiments, density-functional theory (DFT) calculations, and a GRACE universal interatomic potential trained on large DFT databases. They studied two alloys: FCC Al₀.₃CoCrFeNi and B2 Al₃CoCrFeNi.
AI.result: The FCC Al₀.₃CoCrFeNi alloy forms hydrides at ambient temperature above 3 GPa when H₂ is used as pressure-transmitting medium. In contrast, the Al-rich B2 Al₃CoCrFeNi alloy shows no evidence of hydride formation even upon heating at pressures up to 50 GPa. Experiments and calculations demonstrate that aluminum suppresses hydrogen uptake by increasing solution energies and destabilizing interstitial sites. The GRACE potential closely reproduces DFT energetics and shows transferability from dilute limit to hydride-forming regime.
AI.conclusion: Hydrogen solubility in Al-containing HEAs is governed primarily by composition, with Al-driven B2 ordering acting as a strong secondary effect. Aluminum increases solution energies and destabilizes interstitial sites, thereby suppressing hydrogen uptake. The universal potential successfully bridges DFT calculations and experimental observations.

title: Angle-Resolved Berry Curvature via Nonlinear Hall Effect of Ballistic Electrons
summary: Berry curvature fundamentally dictates the topological ground state, anomalous transport and optical properties of quantum materials. However, directly mapping its momentum-space distribution in real materials remains an outstanding experimental challenge. Here, we present an inverse method for reconstructing the abelian Berry curvature of a single band using angle-resolved measurements of the transverse conductance. Our inversion relies on a symmetry-constrained statistical model with two hyperparameters that can be inferred directly from the nonlinear Hall conductance, yielding a parameter-free inversion method. We demonstrate the feasibility of our method using simulated measurements of tight-binding models of WSe$_2$ and $ABC$-stacked trilayer graphene.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an inverse method to reconstruct the abelian Berry curvature of a single band using angle-resolved measurements of transverse conductance. The approach uses a symmetry-constrained statistical model with two hyperparameters that can be directly inferred from nonlinear Hall conductance, enabling parameter-free inversion.
AI.motivation: Directly mapping the momentum-space distribution of Berry curvature in real materials remains an outstanding experimental challenge, despite its fundamental importance in determining topological ground states, anomalous transport, and optical properties of quantum materials.
AI.method: The authors developed an inversion method relying on a symmetry-constrained statistical model with two hyperparameters. These hyperparameters can be inferred directly from nonlinear Hall conductance measurements, yielding a parameter-free inversion approach using angle-resolved measurements of transverse conductance.
AI.result: The feasibility of the method was demonstrated using simulated measurements on tight-binding models of WSe2 and ABC-stacked trilayer graphene.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a practical experimental approach for reconstructing Berry curvature distribution in momentum space, addressing a key challenge in characterizing topological quantum materials.

title: Thermodynamic Discovery of Tetracriticality and Emergent Multicomponent Superconductivity in UTe$_2$
summary: The candidate topological superconductor UTe$_2$ exhibits a complex phase diagram with multiple superconducting states, yet the nature of their coexistence has remained a central mystery. In particular, the apparent intersection of two second-order phase boundaries at a ``triple point'' in the pressure-temperature phase diagram is thermodynamically forbidden, suggesting hidden phase transitions or a fundamental misunderstanding of the superconductivity in UTe$_2$. Here, we use pulse-echo ultrasound to resolve this puzzle by discovering a new phase boundary that is characterized by a unique ``upward jump" in the sound velocity -- direct thermodynamic evidence for a re-entrant phase transition. Our results establish $\left(P^{\star},T^{\star}\right)$ as a tetracrtical point, beyond which the ambient and pressure-induced superconducting order parameters form a multi-component state. We use the measured phase diagram to construct a Ginzburg-Landau theory that shows that strong competition between the two superconducting order parameters drives the re-entrance and leads to phase locking that suppress superconducting fluctuations. These findings provide the definitive magnetic field-temperature-pressure phase diagram and establish a thermodynamic foundation for multi-component -- and potentially topological -- superconductivity in UTe$_2$.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves a thermodynamic puzzle in UTe2 by discovering a re-entrant phase transition using pulse-echo ultrasound, establishing a tetracritical point and a multi-component superconducting state.
AI.motivation: The apparent intersection of two second-order phase boundaries at a "triple point" in the pressure-temperature phase diagram of UTe2 was thermodynamically forbidden, suggesting either hidden phase transitions or a fundamental misunderstanding of the superconductivity in this candidate topological superconductor.
AI.method: The researchers used pulse-echo ultrasound technique to measure sound velocity changes and discover a new phase boundary characterized by a unique "upward jump" in sound velocity. They then constructed a Ginzburg-Landau theory based on the measured phase diagram.
AI.result: The study discovered a new phase boundary with an "upward jump" in sound velocity, providing direct thermodynamic evidence for a re-entrant phase transition. This established (P*, T*) as a tetracritical point where ambient and pressure-induced superconducting order parameters form a multi-component state. The Ginzburg-Landau theory showed that strong competition between the two superconducting order parameters drives the re-entrance and leads to phase locking that suppresses superconducting fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide the definitive magnetic field-temperature-pressure phase diagram and establish a thermodynamic foundation for multi-component and potentially topological superconductivity in UTe2, resolving the long-standing mystery about the coexistence of multiple superconducting states.

title: Interface-dependent Phase Transitions and Ultrafast Hydrogen Superionic Diffusion of H2O Ice
summary: High-pressure experiments using diamond anvils have revealed novel properties and phase behavior of H2O under extreme conditions. When contained in diamond-anvil cells, the H2O samples are usually in direct contact with the diamond anvil. However, the extent to which this interface affects measured pressure-induced properties and behavior, including coexistence lines of ice phases, remains unknown. Combining artificial neural network methods and active learning schemes with large-scale molecular dynamics simulations, we elucidate the interfacial effects on various properties of high-pressure ice phases, including superionic states, solid-solid phase transitions, and melting. The results reveal that the presence of this interface can significantly lower the hydrogen superionic transition temperature. Remarkably, the interface can also induce a spontaneous transition from bcc- to fcc-based ice following the inverse Bain mechanism. Further, we redefined a stability field of bcc and fcc ice below the melting line and predicted the existence of fcc ice at much lower pressures than previously thought. More broadly, the results emphasize the importance of interface effects in understanding a wide range of phenomena reported in experimental studies of ice under pressure, including inconsistencies between theoretical and experimental results of this fundamental system.

AI.tldr: This study uses machine learning combined with molecular dynamics simulations to investigate how the diamond-water interface affects properties of high-pressure ice phases. The researchers found that the interface significantly lowers the hydrogen superionic transition temperature, induces a bcc- to fcc-phase transition via the inverse Bain mechanism, and reveals fcc ice stability at much lower pressures than previously recognized.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the unknown extent to which the diamond-water interface in diamond-anvil cell experiments affects measured pressure-induced properties of water ice, including phase transitions and coexistence lines. This is important because most high-pressure water experiments use diamond-anvil cells where water is in direct contact with diamond, but the interfacial effects remain unexplored.
AI.method: The method combines artificial neural network methods and active learning schemes with large-scale molecular dynamics simulations to study interfacial effects on high-pressure ice phases.
AI.result: The key findings are: (1) The diamond-water interface significantly lowers the hydrogen superionic transition temperature; (2) The interface induces spontaneous bcc- to fcc-ice transition following the inverse Bain mechanism; (3) A revised stability field of bcc and fcc ice below the melting line was redefined; (4) fcc ice exists at much lower pressures than previously thought.
AI.conclusion: Interface effects are crucial in understanding phenomena reported in experimental studies of ice under pressure, particularly inconsistencies between theoretical and experimental results. This work suggests that diamond-anvil cell experiments may need to account for interfacial effects when studying high-pressure ice properties.

authors: Roald J. H. van der Kolk
title: Quantum-Material Josephson Junctions: Unconventional Barriers, Emerging Functionality
summary: Josephson junctions translate quantum phase coherence into an electrical response and underpin superconducting sensors and quantum circuits. In conventional junctions, the barrier acts primarily as a passive weak link, however, when the barrier is a quantum material with its own internal degrees of freedom like magnetism, strong correlations, or switchable polarization, the Josephson effect becomes a sensitive probe of symmetry and many-body physics in the interlayer. Here we review progress in quantum-material Josephson junctions, (QMJJ) focusing on three rapidly advancing barrier families: 1. magnetic barriers, where exchange, noncollinearity, and spin-active scattering enable 0-π-φ ground states, singlet-triplet conversion, and nonreciprocal transport, 2. correlated barriers, where proximity effects acquire many-body character and recent van der Waals Kagome Mott interlayers exhibit field-free Josephson diode behavior, and 3. ferroelectric and multiferroic barriers, where nonvolatile polarization provides an internal control knob and can produce superconducting memory and memristive dynamics.

AI.tldr: This review paper discusses quantum-material Josephson junctions (QMJJs) where the barrier material itself has intrinsic quantum properties (magnetism, correlations, or ferroelectricity), transforming passive weak links into active probes of symmetry and many-body physics. The paper covers three major barrier families: magnetic barriers enabling exotic ground states and spin conversion, correlated barriers showing many-body proximity effects and diode behavior, and ferroelectric barriers offering nonvolatile control and memory functions.
AI.motivation: Conventional Josephson junctions treat barriers as passive elements, but quantum materials with internal degrees of freedom can enable richer physics. The authors aim to review how these quantum-material barriers turn the Josephson effect into a sensitive probe of many-body physics and symmetry.
AI.method: This is a review paper synthesizing progress in three categories of quantum material barriers: (1) magnetic barriers with exchange interactions and spin-active scattering, (2) correlated barriers including van der Waals Kagome Mott materials, and (3) ferroelectric/multiferroic barriers with switchable polarization.
AI.result: Key findings include: magnetic barriers enable 0-π-φ ground states, singlet-triplet conversion, and nonreciprocal transport; correlated van der Waals Kagome Mott interlayers show field-free Josephson diode behavior; ferroelectric barriers provide nonvolatile polarization control for superconducting memory and memristive dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-material Josephson junctions represent a rapidly advancing field where the barrier's intrinsic quantum properties can be leveraged to create novel electronic states, enable memory functions, and provide sensitive probes of many-body physics and symmetry in interlayer materials.

title: Identification of sub-angstrom many-body localization in quantum materials by Bragg scattering phase breaking and ultrafast structural dynamics
summary: Defects, fluctuations, degenerate states and correlated interactions facilitate the emergence of exotic properties in condensed matter systems while also inducing atomic-scale local correlated structures that deviate from the average long-range order. Establishing the structure-property relationship from the perspective of these atomic-scale local correlated structures remains ambiguous and controversial due to the lack of direct methods for identifying such local correlated structures. In this work, based on the photoexcited ultrafast structural response, we propose a Bragg scattering phase breaking regime to identify the sub-angstrom local correlated structures in quantum materials. With this regime, we unambiguously identify the many-body-interaction driven local correlated structures with static off-center Ag displacements of 0 to 0.5 angstrom in the low temperature ground state of AgCrSe2. As temperature rising, these static local correlated structures transform to a dynamic state where the thermal fluctuations overwhelm the multiple localized quantum states, signifying the strong anharmonicity of the local structures. The state-of-the-art density functional theory simulation well reproduces the intrinsic many-body-interaction driven local correlated structures. These unique local correlated structures evidence the first many-body localization with topological order characteristic in real material systems and provide a unified scenario for the versatile quantum properties in single crystalline AgCrSe2. Our work not only offers a universal approach to characterize sub-angstrom local correlated structures across a wide range of quantum materials but also deepens our understanding of the fundamental mechanism behind exotic properties from the perspective of atomic-scale local correlated structures.

AI.tldr: This work proposes a Bragg scattering phase breaking regime to identify sub-angstrom local correlated structures in quantum materials, applying it to AgCrSe2 to reveal many-body-interaction driven local correlated structures with static off-center Ag displacements of 0-0.5 Å that transform from static to dynamic states with increasing temperature.
AI.motivation: Establishing structure-property relationships from atomic-scale local correlated structures remains ambiguous due to lack of direct identification methods. The paper aims to address this gap by developing a technique to identify these local correlated structures that deviate from long-range order.
AI.method: The researchers used photoexcited ultrafast structural response combined with state-of-the-art density functional theory simulations to identify and characterize local correlated structures in AgCrSe2.
AI.result: The study identified many-body-interaction driven local correlated structures with static off-center Ag displacements of 0 to 0.5 angstrom in the low temperature ground state of AgCrSe2. With temperature increase, these static structures transform to a dynamic state where thermal fluctuations overwhelm localized quantum states, indicating strong anharmonicity.
AI.conclusion: These findings evidence the first many-body localization with topological order characteristic in real material systems and provide a unified scenario for quantum properties in AgCrSe2. The work offers a universal approach to characterize sub-angstrom local correlated structures across quantum materials.

title: Polaron-mediated anisotropic exchange in 2D magnets
summary: Two-dimensional (2D) magnets offer a rich platform for exploring emergent spin phenomena due to their unique and diverse magnetic properties. Beyond intrinsic magnetism, external manipulation$\unicode{x2013}$such as defect engineering, molecular adsorption, or charge doping$\unicode{x2013}$offers powerful routes to control their magnetic behavior. In this work, we demonstrate that localized electron polarons provide an effective means to modulate magnetism in 2D magnets. Using first-principles calculations, we investigate polaron formation in monolayer MnPS$_3$ and compute the resulting changes in magnetic exchange interactions. Our results reveal that polarons can locally break magnetic symmetry and induce anisotropic exchange couplings, highlighting a novel mechanism for tuning magnetic textures. This insight opens promising pathways for designing atomic-scale control of magnetism, with potential impact on spintronic technologies.

AI.tldr: This work demonstrates that localized electron polarons can effectively modulate magnetism in 2D magnets, specifically monolayer MnPS3, using first-principles calculations to show polaron-induced magnetic symmetry breaking and anisotropic exchange couplings.
AI.motivation: Two-dimensional magnets exhibit rich emergent spin phenomena, and external manipulation methods (defect engineering, molecular adsorption, charge doping) offer powerful routes to control their magnetic behavior. However, a novel mechanism for tuning magnetic textures at the atomic scale remains to be explored.
AI.method: First-principles calculations were employed to investigate polaron formation in monolayer MnPS3 and compute the resulting changes in magnetic exchange interactions.
AI.result: Polarons can locally break magnetic symmetry and induce anisotropic exchange couplings, providing a novel mechanism for tuning magnetic textures.
AI.conclusion: This insight opens promising pathways for designing atomic-scale control of magnetism, with potential impact on spintronic technologies.

title: Chiral-Induced Spin Selectivity Effect in a 1 nm Thin 1,1'-Binaphthyl-2,2'-diyl Hydrogenphosphate Self-Assembled Monolayer on Nickel Oxide
summary: The chiral-induced spin selectivity (CISS) effect describes an observed correlation between the orientation of an electron spin transported or transferred through a molecule and that molecule's chirality. Suitable molecules are usually arranged as self-assembled monolayers (SAMs), and the primary CISS systems are based on multiple nanometer-long biomolecules exhibiting helical chirality. Aside from these typically thiolate-anchored molecules, phosphonic and phosphoric acid SAMs may well become significant for those CISS applications that require a more robust molecular coupling to metal oxide surfaces. In this work, we report on our studies, employing the aromatic, low-molecular-mass, axially chiral organophosphoric acid derivative 1,1'-binaphthyl-2,2'-diyl hydrogenphosphate (BNP). Grown as a roughly 1 nm thin SAM on top of a NiOx/Ni substrate, a strong circular dichroism signal indicates that the thin films preserved chirality. The CISS response exhibits a high magnetoresistance with a spin polarization of 50-80% when measured using magnetic-conductive atomic force microscopy. For biases above 0.5 V, the magnetoresistance curves could be well fitted to the Fowler-Nordheim (FN) tunneling model. Using a minimal FN model, we determined that, depending on the magnetization direction and the handedness of the molecules, electrons of a certain spin direction face an effective tunneling barrier at high bias, which is either 80 % higher or 40 % lower compared to the barrier for electrons of the opposite spin direction. Due to the small size of the molecules, their compatibility with oxide materials, and their commercial availability, they are excellent candidates for the realization of novel (nanoscale) organic spintronic devices.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates CISS effect in small axially chiral organophosphoric acid molecules (BNP) self-assembled on NiOx/Ni, achieving 50-80% spin polarization through Fowler-Nordheim tunneling analysis, showing potential for organic spintronic applications.
AI.motivation: The research aims to develop alternative CISS systems using phosphonic/phosphoric acid SAMs instead of traditional thiolate-anchored molecules, particularly for applications requiring robust molecular coupling to metal oxide surfaces. The motivation is to find smaller, more compatible molecules for organic spintronic devices.
AI.method: The study used axially chiral organophosphoric acid derivative 1,1'-binaphthyl-2,2'-diyl hydrogenphosphate (BNP) to form ~1 nm thin SAMs on NiOx/Ni substrates. They employed magnetic-conductive atomic force microscopy (MC-AFM) to measure CISS response and used Fowler-Nordheim tunneling model to analyze magnetoresistance data.
AI.result: The SAMs showed strong circular dichroism signal confirming preserved chirality. High magnetoresistance with 50-80% spin polarization was observed. FN tunneling model fitting revealed that depending on molecular handedness and magnetization direction, electrons of certain spin direction face tunneling barriers either 80% higher or 40% lower than opposite spin electrons.
AI.conclusion: BNP molecules are excellent candidates for organic spintronic devices due to their small molecular size, compatibility with oxide materials, and commercial availability. The study demonstrates that phosphoric acid-based SAMs can serve as effective CISS systems with strong spin polarization.

title: Simulating the influence of stoichiometry on the spectral emissivity of Mo$_x$Si$_y$ thin films
summary: In this work, we simulate the spectral emissivity of various stoichiometric crystal phases of Mo$_x$Si$_y$ compounds using density functional perturbation theory. The dielectric function, including electronic and ionic contributions, is calculated for each phase. We use the bulk properties obtained to simulate the optical absorption spectrum originating from the compound in thin film ($\sim$20 nm) form. We find that most thin films of Mo$_x$Si$_y$ are metallic, however, our results indicate that their emissivity is not simply correlated with the Mo content. For hot metallic films at around 900 K, we predict a maximal emissivity between 5-10 nm thickness. Our results are in good qualitative agreement with experiments, confirming that the emissivity of hexagonal MoSi$_2$ is much lower than in the tetragonal phase. This is related to the small band gap (hexagonal MoSi$_2$) and low density of states at the Fermi level (tetragonal MoSi$_2$). Furthermore, test calculations on defected MoSi$_2$ demonstrate that the infrared emissivity of MoSi$_2$ thin films can be substantially increased by introducing defects.

AI.tldr: This paper uses density functional perturbation theory to simulate spectral emissivity of Mo_xSi_y compounds in thin film form (~20 nm). The study reveals that emissivity depends on crystal structure and thickness, not simply Mo content, with maximal emissivity at 5-10 nm for films at 900 K. Defects can substantially increase infrared emissivity.
AI.motivation: To understand and predict the emissivity properties of molybdenum silicide compounds for thin film applications, addressing the lack of simple correlation between Mo content and emissivity observed in experiments.
AI.method: Density functional perturbation theory to calculate dielectric functions (electronic and ionic contributions), simulate optical absorption spectra, and analyze bulk properties of various stoichiometric crystal phases of Mo_xSi_y compounds.
AI.result: Most Mo_xSi_y thin films are metallic. Emissivity is not simply correlated with Mo content. Maximal emissivity occurs at 5-10 nm thickness for metallic films at ~900 K. Hexagonal MoSi2 has much lower emissivity than tetragonal phase due to small band gap (hexagonal) and low density of states at Fermi level (tetragonal). Introducing defects substantially increases infrared emissivity.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical results are in good qualitative agreement with experiments, confirming that crystal structure and defects significantly influence emissivity properties of MoSi2 thin films, providing guidance for designing materials with tailored infrared emission characteristics.

title: Substrate-controlled nucleation and growth kinetics in ultrathin Bi$_2$Te$_3$ films
summary:
Metal chalcogenides are promising layered topological materials, yet their electronic performance is often limited by parasitic bulk conduction arising from defects that introduce excess carriers and shift the Fermi level out of the topological regime. Controlling early-stage growth and defect formation is therefore essential for suppressing bulk transport and enhancing surface-state conduction. Here we investigate ultrathin Bi2Te3 films grown by pulsed laser deposition on substrates spanning van der Waals, lattice-matched, and amorphous regimes to determine how substrate-dependent nucleation pathways influence defect formation and electronic transport.
  Phase-pure, c-axis-oriented Bi2Te3 forms on all substrates, but the growth morphology varies strongly. Layered growth with well-defined quintuple-layer terraces is governed primarily by substrate roughness rather than lattice match: atomically smooth mica and step-terraced SrTiO3 yield continuous terraces, whereas rougher BaF2 and amorphous Si3N4 produce island-structured films. Between the two smooth substrates, the higher surface energy of SrTiO3 enhances adatom adsorption and nucleation density, promoting rapid vertical growth and early Te depletion.
  Transport measurements reveal n-type conduction with carrier densities of 10e19-10e20 cm-3. The highest carrier density occurs for films on SrTiO3, consistent with defect formation during high-density nucleation, whereas mobility correlates with structural coherence and terrace formation. Weak anti-localization signatures confirm phase-coherent transport in films on mica and SrTiO3. These results show that substrate roughness and nucleation density provide key levers for controlling defect formation and strengthening topological surface transport in Bi2Te3 thin films.

AI.tldr: This study investigates ultrathin Bi2Te3 topological insulator films grown by pulsed laser deposition on various substrates to understand how substrate-dependent nucleation pathways influence defect formation and electronic transport. The researchers found that substrate roughness, rather than lattice match, governs growth morphology, and that controlling nucleation density is key to suppressing bulk conduction and enhancing surface-state transport.
AI.motivation: Metal chalcogenides like Bi2Te3 are promising topological materials, but their electronic performance is limited by parasitic bulk conduction caused by defects that introduce excess carriers and shift the Fermi level out of the topological regime. The researchers aimed to control early-stage growth and defect formation to enhance surface-state conduction.
AI.method: The researchers grew ultrathin Bi2Te3 films by pulsed laser deposition on substrates spanning van der Waals (mica), lattice-matched (SrTiO3), and amorphous (BaF2, Si3N4) regimes. They systematically studied how substrate-dependent nucleation pathways influence defect formation and electronic transport through structural and transport measurements.
AI.result: Phase-pure, c-axis-oriented Bi2Te3 forms on all substrates. Growth morphology depends on substrate roughness: atomically smooth mica and SrTiO3 yield continuous terraces, while rougher BaF2 and Si3N4 produce island-structured films. All films show n-type conduction with carrier densities of 10^19-10^20 cm^-3. The highest carrier density occurs for films on SrTiO3, consistent with defect formation during high-density nucleation. Mobility correlates with structural coherence and terrace formation. Weak anti-localization signatures confirm phase-coherent transport in films on mica and SrTiO3.
AI.conclusion: Substrate roughness and nucleation density provide key levers for controlling defect formation and strengthening topological surface transport in Bi2Te3 thin films. This work demonstrates that understanding and controlling early-stage growth pathways is essential for optimizing the electronic performance of topological insulator materials.

title: Mechanistic Insights into Enhanced Alkaline Oxygen Evolution on Zn-Al Alloy Electrodes
summary: Electrochemical water electrolysis, which produces clean energy carriers to mitigate carbon emissions, lacks suitable, low-cost electrodes for efficient oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in alkaline water splitting. To address this challenge, we developed Zn-Al alloy electrodes with varying Al contents up to 20 wt.% via powder metallurgy method and conducted electrochemical measurements of the OER in alkaline solution to investigate their catalytic performance. We also performed first-principles calculations to examine their thermodynamic phase stability and electronic structures. Both theoretical and experimental results indicated that incorporating $\geq 20$ wt.% Al into Zn led to thermodynamic phase instability and secondary-phase segregation in Al-rich regions, limiting reaction kinetics and reducing catalytic efficiency. Although the Al content of 5 wt.% into Zn exhibited favorable thermodynamic and electronic characteristics, but its electrochemical performance was inefficient and poor due to inadequate reaction active sites on the surface. In contrast, the 10 wt.% and 15 wt.% Al into Zn showed approximately three- and two-fold increases in anodic exchange current density relative to pure Zn, respectively. Additionally, the anodic overpotential losses ($η_{0,a}$) measured at a current density of 12 mAcm$^{-2}$ were 0.240 V for Zn$_{0.9}$Al$_{0.1}$ and 0.5603 V for Zn$_{0.85}$Al$_{0.15}$, significantly lower than that of pure Zn ($η_{0,a} = 1.086$ V). While Zn$_{0.9}$Al$_{0.1}$ and Zn$_{0.85}$Al$_{0.15}$ showed similar charge transfer resistance ($R_{\rm CT}$), Zn$_{0.9}$Al$_{0.1}$ demonstrated superior reaction kinetics and lower $η_{0,a}$ across all samples tested. Furthermore, the improved kinetics and reduced overpotential of the Zn-Al alloys favorably compare with those of other transition-metal-based catalysts, including Fe-Co-Ni-Mo alloys and Fe-doped CuO.

AI.tldr: This study develops Zn-Al alloy electrodes via powder metallurgy for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in alkaline water splitting. Both experimental and first-principles calculations reveal that 10 wt.% Al content provides optimal performance with significantly reduced overpotential (0.240 V vs 1.086 V for pure Zn) and improved reaction kinetics.
AI.motivation: Electrochemical water electrolysis lacks suitable, low-cost electrodes for efficient OER in alkaline water splitting. The researchers aimed to develop cost-effective Zn-Al alloy electrodes to address this challenge.
AI.method: Zn-Al alloy electrodes with varying Al contents (up to 20 wt.%) were fabricated via powder metallurgy method. Electrochemical measurements were conducted in alkaline solution, complemented by first-principles calculations to examine thermodynamic phase stability and electronic structures.
AI.result: 10 wt.% Al (Zn₀.₉Al₀.₁) showed the best performance with ~3-fold increase in anodic exchange current density and η₀,a = 0.240 V at 12 mA cm⁻², compared to pure Zn (η₀,a = 1.086 V). Higher Al content (≥20 wt.%) caused phase instability, while 5 wt.% had insufficient active sites.
AI.conclusion: Zn₀.₉Al₀.₁ demonstrates superior OER catalytic performance with reduced overpotential and improved kinetics, favorably comparing with other transition-metal-based catalysts like Fe-Co-Ni-Mo alloys and Fe-doped CuO.
